observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor directed the Local Development Council (LDC) and the OIC-MPDC on the preparation and procurement-and-implementation readiness of the project. The Municipal Mayor also directed the OIC-MPDC to assist the Punong Barangays on the facilitation and preparation of permits and necessary documents.
observations.reason: The project's implementation is hampered by situations or events beyond the Municipality's control.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
• Municipal Assessor to facilitate the titling of the subject lots to establish ownership over them and avoid contingent claims that may arise in the future in compliance with Section 148 of COA Circular No. 92-386 and
• Municipal Accountant to determine the details of the unidentified land by gathering the relevant supporting documents from the Municipal Treasurer and Assessor’s Office for the fair presentation of the land account in the financial statements pursuant to Paragraph 27 of IPSAS 1.
For preparation of Tracking Sheet (RTS) for approval of the Regional Director.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Assessor to facilitate the titling process for the subject lots to secure ownership and prevent future claims.
The Municipal Accountant to determine the details of the unidentified land by gathering the relevant supporting documents from the Municipal Treasurer and Assessor’s Office for the fair presentation of the land account in the financial statements.
observations.reason:
The process of titling was still on going.
Due to passage of time, it was not doable anymore to identify the details of the parcel of lots.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office (MDRRO) Operations Head to fully implement all the planned PPAs and optimally utilize the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF) in order to build a disaster resilient community and reduce disaster risks.
observations.reason: For CY 2023, 80.28 percent of the appropriation for LDRRMF-Mitigation Fund was not utilized by the Municipality. Reiterated in Observation No. 5, Part II of this AAR.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to submit to the Audit Team proof that goods and services are accepted or rendered and otherwise prepare an adjusting entry Debiting the Accounts Payable and Crediting the corresponding supplier/creditor account/s.
Henceforth, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that the recording of Accounts Payable is in accordance with Section 4(s) of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant prepared an adjusting entry reverting the Accounts Payable to Surplus on June 30, 2023, while the remaining P1,464,841.20 was still included in the Accounts Payable balance as of December 31, 2023 since these were provided with proof that goods/services are accepted or rendered and otherwise prepare an adjusting entry Debiting the Accounts Payable and Crediting the supplier/creditor account/s.
observations.reason: As of December 31, 2023, the recorded Accounts Payable totaling P11,157,423.70 were not supported with complete documentation. In addition, the supporting documents for the total amount of P12,962,169.14 were not provided to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor rescind the contract of the 62 unfinished contractors, forfeit the contractor’s performance security and takeover the project or award the same to a qualified contractor through negotiated contract.

We also recommended the issuance of a Blacklisting Order on the concerned contractor disqualifying the erring contractors from participating in the bidding of all government projects in conformity with Appendix 17 of the IRR of RA 9184.

Henceforth, instruct the Municipal Engineering Office (MEO) to ensure that the on-going infrastructure projects are completed within the target completion dates as specified in the terms and conditions of the contract.
observations.managementAction: Letters of Recission were sent to the contractors in November 2023. Despite receipt of the letters, the contractors did not reply nor dispute/contest the recission. The MEO has been instructed by the Municipal Mayor to ensure that on-going projects are completed on-time.
observations.reason: Not Issuance of Blacklisting Order on the concerned contractor disqualifying the erring contractors was still not made.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) to ensure that all development projects to be funded out of the 20% Development Fund are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation ready.
observations.managementAction: MPDC has been instructed by the Municipal Mayor to ensure that all development projects to be funded out of the 20% Development Fund are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation ready.
observations.reason: Development projects with a total appropriation of P41,660,032.24 and P5,000,000.00 under the Current Appropriations and Continuing Appropriations, respectively, were still not implemented since the allocated budget for the project was insufficient due to the late submission of Program of Works and layout by the MEO to the Municipal Budget Office.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the CAD:
Immediately issue a final demand letter to the respective AOs for the immediate submission of liquidation reports and their supporting documents; and
In case of failure to liquidate, cause the filing of appropriate disciplinary actions on these erring AOs pursuant to Item 5.1.3, COA Circular 
No. 97-002.

We recommended that the Management require:
The CGSD and CAD to complete the details in the RPCPPE and prepare necessary reconciliation between their records; and
The CAD to prepare the adjusting entries on the affected accounts, after the conduct of reconciliation procedures pursuant to the provisions under Letter I of Item A, Annex 49 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended and the Management agreed to require:
The QCED to submit to CAD the documents for the terminated projects and the inspection report on status of projects recorded as CIP without details/undetermined status; and
The CAD to prepare the necessary adjusting entries to reclassify the terminated projects and projects without details to their appropriate accounts upon receipt of the supporting documents from the QCED pursuant to Paragraphs 27 of IPSAS 1, Section 50 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I and COA Circular No. 2015-009.

We recommended that the Management:
Require CAD and CGSD to reconcile the discrepancies between the GL and RPCI for the inventory accounts and prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to correct the respective balances;
Direct the CAD to:
avoid the recording of inventory acquisition as an outright expense but record them first in the appropriate inventory accounts in accordance with the perpetual inventory method; and
prepare and maintain the appropriate SLCs for each kind of commodity/stock of DMI and MDLSI for proper monitoring and accurate inventory records pursuant to Paragraph 27 of IPSAS 1, Paragraph 44 of IPSAS 12, and Section 114 of the MNGAS for LGUs Volume I.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Identify all the dormant receivable and payable accounts for 10 years or more, and prepare the necessary schedules and documents as prescribed in Item 6.3 of COA Circular No. 2023-008 to initiate the process of derecognition of these accounts; and
Require the CAD to file the request for authority to derecognize within one year from the effectivity as provided in Item 8 of the Circular.

We recommended that Management require the CAD to make the necessary adjustments to correct the overstatement on the balance of Other Deferred Credits and understatement in the balances Government Equity accounts pursuant to Paragraph 27 of IPSAS 1 and Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-009.

We recommended and Management agreed, moving forward, to initially record the receipt of inventories and as expense the when consumed or distributed in compliance with Section 114, Chapter 7 of MNGAS for LGUs and Item 44 of IPSAS 12.

We recommended that the Management direct the Hospital Directors to:
Plan judiciously the procurement of D&M based on a prioritization matrix, taking into consideration the historical data from the past years, which include movement of stocks (whether fast-moving or 
slow-moving), frequency of prescription (based on therapeutic use), and other essential factors as necessary; and
Implement an inventory management system which considers the timing of deliveries, storage, and re-order points to lessen the occurrence of inventory stockouts, thereby minimizing the number of unfilled D&M pursuant to Article XIII, Section 11 of the 1987 Philippine Constitution and the Hospital Pharmacy Management Manual of the DOH.
We recommended and Management agreed to ensure that all Aos of NDH and RMBGH are covered with fidelity bond pursuant to Sections 4.2 and 4.5 of the Treasury Circular No. 02-2009.

We recommended and the Management agreed to:
Require the Local Finance Committee to revisit the QC Revenue Code and update the necessary provisions on the interest for the delinquent RPT pursuant to Section 255 of RA No. 7160; and
Propose to the City Council the review and enactment of an Ordinance for the revisions on interest to be included in the QC Revenue Code.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Ensure that projects undergo extensive coordination with the target beneficiaries through public consultations and site inspection to avoid non-implementation of PPAs caused by issues on right of way and the like;
Follow the procurement timelines set in the Annual Procurement Plan for these projects or conduct Early Procurement Activities to deliver the PPAs on time;
Conduct thorough planning and extensive research to consider all factors that might affect the implementation and timelines of the planned PPAs to minimize, if not avoid, plan revisions, work suspension orders, and time extension orders that cause delays in the implementation of the PPAs; and
Direct the concerned officials of the City to observe the guidelines on the appropriation and utilization of the 20% DF under item 3.2.3 of 
DILG-DBM JMC No. 2020-1 dated November 4, 2020, and item 5 of 
DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 17, 2017.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Require the QCDRRMO to coordinate with other offices/departments involved in the DRRM activities to consolidate PPAs that are similar in nature in preparation of the LDRRMFIP to avoid duplication of projects;
Consider augmentation of existing workforce, through hiring of additional personnel or commissioning the services of force multipliers, to effectively execute the City’s diverse DRRM programs.
Provide detailed information on the actual accomplishments on the programs conducted to provide a basis for evaluation of the outcomes with the planned activities to properly monitor the success of the DRRM programs implemented;
Monitor the implementation of all planned LDRRM PPAs and realign priorities, if necessary, through the preparation of a revised LDRRMFIP to ensure the achievement of the City’s goal and objectives in mitigating the impact of disasters; and
Observe the guidelines regarding LDRRM PPAs under NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1, Section 1, Rule 18 of the Implementing Rules, and Regulations (IRR) of RA No.10121 and Section VI.6 of 
COA Memorandum No. 2014-009.

We recommended and Management agreed to endeavor maximizing the utilization/implementation of the GAD Plan and Budget to attain its objective in accordance with Item 2.3 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA 
JMC No. 2013-01 dated July 18, 2013, as amended by PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2016-01.

We recommended and Management agreed that:
The CAD to –
Further analyze the transactions to identify any unremitted contributions and loan amortization balances of affected employees; thereafter, conduct regular reconciliation of amounts withheld and remitted to avoid recurrence of over or 
under-remittance and immediately adjust any differences in the books;
Coordinate with the Human Resources Department to regularly reconcile their records and ensure accurate remittance of Pag-IBIG contributions and loan amortizations and prepare the necessary adjustment of discrepancies in the books; and
Ensure that all deducted contributions of Pag-IBIG employer and employees share are promptly recorded in the books and subsequently remitted to the HDMF; and
The Human Resource Management Department (HRMD) to require the employees to update their records and provide details to the CAD of the required information to avoid future rejection of remittances by the 
Pag-IBIG system; and
The CAD and HRMD strictly comply with Section 3 of Rule VII of Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA No. 9769 to avoid exposing the City employees to unnecessary penalties the HDMF may impose.

We recommended and Management agreed to require:
CAD FAAD and PBRD to:
Conduct further analysis and retrieval of necessary supporting documents to substantiate and establish the final balances of the SLs to correct the negative balances reported in the books;
Perform a reconciliation on the records of FAAD and PBRD to identify the discrepancies of the SLs and prepare the necessary adjusting entries to correct the  balance of the Due to BIR account; and
HRMD and CAD to:
Reconcile their records every month to promptly establish the actual amount to be withheld from employees, suppliers and contractors for immediate recording and remittance to the BIR.

We recommended that Management ensure regular remittance of premium contributions in accordance with Sections 18(b), Rule III, Title III of the Revised IRR of the National Health Insurance Act, RA No. 7875, as amended by RA No. 9241 and RA No. 10606 and PhilHealth Circular No. 0001, s. 2014.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Reassess the current human resource strategy of the City and prioritize the hiring of permanent plantilla positions over temporary ones to ensure stability in the hospital’s workforce; and
Moving forward, to take into consideration the inclusion of medical courses in the City University and require its graduates to serve in the City Hospitals and health centers to augment the workforce deficiencies.

We recommended that Management:
Coordinate with DBS provider for the immediate submission of the required reports to properly record the related expenses of the P74,971,000.82 scholarship grants/stipend to beneficiaries in the books and prepare the necessary adjusting entries on the affected accounts accordingly;
Make amendments to the agreement with DBS provider to include the necessary provision under Section 5.2(f), such as but not limited to 
(i) the required number of days to submit the officially generated report of successful and unsuccessful crediting of scholarship grants/stipends to students, reckoning from the date of requests from QCYDO, and (ii) corresponding liquidated damage clause in favor of the City in case of non-performance; and

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Accountant immediately enforce the settlement of all audit suspensions, disallowances, and charges in compliance with Sections 5.4 and 7.1.1, Chapter II of 
COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009, and Management be more vigilant in the implementation of the existing accounting and auditing rules and regulations in the processing of claims to avoid possible audit suspensions and disallowances.

We recommended that the Management advise the General Services Officer – designate and the Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of Biological Asset and prepare the RPCBA and complete the maintenance of BAPC in accordance with Government Accounting Manual for LGUs. Likewise, the Municipal Accountant to present the balance of Biological Asset using the recognition and measurement criteria of IPSAS 27.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Management advise the MGSO to conduct the physical count on inventories so that they can prepare and submit to the Office of the Auditor the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) for CY 2024.
We reiterated our recommendation that the General Services Officer prepare and maintain Property Cards and regularly reconcile the balances to the accounting unit.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer submit to the Municipal Accountant the breakdown of daily collections into its specific revenue/income account as a basis for the recording of daily collections by the latter based on the specific revenue/income account.

We recommended that the Management advise the Municipal Accountant to make a representation to the Accountant of DOH and DSWD to reconcile the balances shown in the confirmation letters and the balance per Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Management direct the Municipal Accountant to maintain special account in the general fund for the 20% Development Fund. Likewise, direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer to reconcile the balances between the adjusted cash in bank – 20% Development Fund and the balance per Registry of the Municipal Budget Office as of December 31, 2023.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer immediately transfer the amount of ₱383,655.01 from the 20% Development Fund account to Trust Fund account. Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to strictly observe the charging of disbursement to proper funds to avoid depletion of cash availability for each fund of the Municipality.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management require the Municipal Treasurer make the necessary transfer of fund from General Fund to Trust Fund amounting to ₱9,004,541.51. Likewise, strictly monitor the balance of cash in bank prior to disbursement of funds so as not to incur cash overdraft.

We recommended that the Liquidating Officer thru the Municipal Treasurer:
to follow the procedure for the proper reporting of collections by the Barangay Treasurer deputized to collect community tax; and
to submit immediately to the Office of the Auditor the duplicate copies of the community tax certificate (BIR Form 0016) remitted by the Barangay Treasurers as well as the copy of Official Receipt (AF51) used to acknowledge the receipt of collections.

We recommended that the Collecting Officers remit their collections daily regardless of the amount collected to the Liquidating Officer who shall deposit the said amount to the authorized government depository bank in compliance to Sections 26 and 31 of COA Circular No. 92-382 dated July 3, 1992 to avoid the exposure of government funds to risks of possible loss, theft, misuse or misappropriation.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Treasurer, being the head of Treasury Office, and the Liquidating Officer to deposit all collections as required by the rules and regulations. Likewise, proper handling of collections and its deposits be strictly observed and deposits should be attributed/traced from the collected amounts. Moreover, review the recently agreed deposit schedule scheme and consider the level of cash on hand to prevent possible misappropriation, theft and loss of funds.
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer strictly adhere to the provisions on the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.

We recommended that the Management advise the above officials and employees to liquidate their cash advances immediately to avoid any legal actions that may arise due to non-liquidation of the above-mentioned cash advances. Likewise, submit immediately to the Office of the Auditor the liquidation report including all its supporting documents upon liquidation.

We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant refrain from using the account Due from Officers and Employees in recording cash advances granted that were not related to official travel.
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer maintain a separate cashbook under Trust Fund and Special Education Fund to monitor the balances of each fund at a given period.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant reconcile their books at least quarterly in order to adjust any variances that may occur and prepare the necessary adjusting entry to reconcile the variance noted, if any.

We recommended Management the following:
Formulate more realistic socio-economic and environmental programs and projects and prioritize their implementation according to the needs of the constituents to avoid constant reprogramming and reversion;
Focus on those projects and activities proposed or recommended by the members of the Municipal Planning and Development Council that are endemic to their area of jurisdiction that will fit the genuine capability of the Municipality to undertake such projects directed to yield and precise Annual Investment Plan; and
Require the Executive Committee to ensure that the decisions of the council are faithfully carried out and implemented.

We recommended that the Management ensure that all development projects are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready in order to complete them within the target completion date to maximize the benefit for its intended beneficiaries. We further recommended that the Management implement the improvement of the Multi-purpose building for the benefit of its constituents.

We recommended that the Management allocate budget on the conduct of Community Based Monitoring System and consider the DBM DOF DILG Joint Memorandum Circular on the use of 20% Development Fund, for the effective and efficient delivery of necessary services to the constituents.

We recommended that the Management make a realignment on the appropriated fund on the maintenance of emergency vehicle to disaster preparedness activity following the provisions set by NDRRMC, DBM and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013 to forestall the issuance of Notice of Disallowance. We further recommended that maintenance of emergency vehicles be charged against the general fund of the Municipality. Likewise, refrain from appropriating funds to unrelated projects and activities of disaster risk reduction and management.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the GAD Focal Person fast track the establishment of GAD sex-disaggregated database to support the gender related issues identified and prioritized in the GAD Plan.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Bids and Awards Committee to prepare and submit to the GPPB the Procurement Monitoring Report (PMR) within the time frame stated in Section 12.2 of the IRR of R.A. No. 9184 as reiterated in GPPB Resolution No. 11-2020, dated May 20, 2020 for monitoring and compliance with the provisions of the Procurement Law.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Engineer to prepare and submit at the beginning of the year to the Audit Team the List of all on-going government projects, programs/activities (PPAs) and those that are to be implemented during the year in compliance with COA Circular No. 2013-004.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Management through the Municipal Accountant submit the necessary reports on timely basis and strictly comply with the provisions in COA Circular No 2009-006 and PD 1445 in order to avoid imposition of the sanctions provided therein.

We reiterated that the Municipal Accountant:
prepare necessary adjusting entry to the affected accounts to ensure fair presentation of financial statements;
cause the transfer of unexpended balance of DRRMF other than those appropriated for capital outlays to the Trust Fund, and record the same as Trust Liabilities – DRRMF; and
abide with the guidelines set forth under Items 5.1.10 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

We reiterated that the Municipal Accountant:
prepare the necessary adjusting entry to the affected accounts to ensure fair presentation of financial statements, i.e. – debit Trust Liability – DRRMF; credit – Prior Period Adjustment;
transfer the unexpended balance of DRRMF other than those appropriated for capital outlays to the Trust Fund and recognized as Trust Liabilities – DRRMF; and
abide with the guidelines set forth under Items 5.1.10 and 5.1.11 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.
We recommended the Accountable Officer to continue the proper handling of government funds pursuant to regulations.
We recommended that the Accountable Officer ensure accurate computation of accountability. Henceforth, strict adherence to P.D. No. 1445 is enjoined.
We recommended the Accountable Officer to uphold the proper handling of government funds according to relevant regulations to ensure accountability and transparency in financial transactions.

We recommended that management:
ensure that trust funds are used exclusively to the purpose/s for which they were granted following the provisions of Section 4(3) of P.D. No. 1445 and Sections 305(e) and 309(b) of R.A. No. 7160; and
require the settlement of long outstanding receivables to augment the deficient cash under trust fund.

We recommended that management require the:
Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical inventory of Property, Plant, and Equipment (PPE) and Investment Property. This should include a reconciliation of the physical count with the property and accounting records, as required by Chapter 4 of the Handbook on Property & Supply Management System and Paragraph 4 of Section 114 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I. Any discrepancies should be thoroughly investigated, cleared, and reconciled immediately and written explanations should be obtained from accountable and responsible persons if necessary.
The implementation of the procedures on one time cleansing of PPE accounts is recommended per COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020;
Accounting Office and General Services Office to enhance documentation and record-keeping practices related to PPE and Investment Property by preparing and continuously maintaining and updating PPE ledger cards and property cards, respectively. Both offices should ensure that all movements of PPE are properly recorded and documented to facilitate accurate reconciliation and reporting; and
The members of the Inventory Committee to undergo training on property and supply management, if necessary.

We reiterated to Management our previous audit recommendation that:
the Municipal Engineer provide the Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer with the complete description and segregation of road components for those with data on construction cost. For those not posted in the registry but without identified cost and those included in the RPCRNS but not in the registry, the fair value must be used in determining the cost of road components and thereafter shall provide the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects;
the Municipal Accountant depreciate the carrying amount of existing roads and its components to reflect the asset in its used condition and accordingly adjust the amount recorded in the Road Network account supported by the RPCRNS, Registry Infrastructure for Roads and working paper on the determination of the depreciated replacement cost per registry. Subsequently, at the end of each year, a lapsing schedule for the computation of depreciation for each depreciable road component must be prepared as basis for recording depreciation expenses and the required Local Road Network Ledger Card for each road network shall likewise be maintained; and
the MGSO at the end or every accounting period to render a Report on Local Road Network using the pro-forma schedule in Annex A of same Circular and maintain local road inventory, Road Map and complete Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components.

We commended management for its high implementation rate of PPAs under the 20% Development Fund, ensuring the optimum utilization thereof, and timely delivery of intended benefits for the constituents of the municipality. Henceforth, management is recommended to:
continuously ensure that the development projects that may be included under the 20% DF shall be those that are necessary, appropriate, or incidental to efficient and effective local governance, and those which are essential to the promotion of the general welfare of the people; and
ensure that the development projects to be funded out of the 20% DF are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready.
We recommended that management cause the immediate return of all unused balances to source agencies or request authority for the continued use of funds for other identified projects of the LGU.

We recommended that Management:
continuously ensure the allocation and utilization of LDRRMF pursuant to the policies and guidelines primarily embodied in NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1;
ensure efficient and effective implementation of all programmed projects identified during the budget year to optimize the utilization of the fund and to assure that the goals and objectives of reducing disaster risk and enhancing disaster preparedness and response capabilities be achieved; and
continuously comply with pertinent laws, rules and regulations in the recording, utilization and reporting of LDRRMF.

We reiterated our previous audit recommendation that:
the Municipal Accountant comply with the provision Section 114 of P.D. No 1445 and Section 4 (e) of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume I, so that all transactions would be properly documented and recorded. With this, all transactions can be readily verified and reconciled with the amount appearing in the financial statements. Thus, the accuracy and reliability of the financial statements will be achieved; and
the Management consider hiring more personnel to augment the staff requirements of the Accounting Office.

We reiterated our previous audit recommendation that:
the Municipal Accountant verify and validate the existence of these receivables, determine the debtors, and assess their collectability. If deemed uncollectible, request authority to write-off the dormant receivable accounts following COA Circular No. 2016-005, as modified by COA Circular No. 2023-008 dated August 17, 2023; and
ensure that subsidiary ledgers are maintained for receivables in accordance with Section 4(e) of the NGAS to provide detailed information on accounts and facilitate collection efforts in the future.

To ensure fair presentation of the financial statements and compliance with accounting standards and regulations, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
analyze the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts, trace back differences and clarify the nature of the accounts;
prepare the necessary adjusting entries to bring the correct balances of the accounts; and
maintain subsidiary ledgers as necessary and include disclosure in the Notes to FS for transparency.

We recommended that the Local School Board, being the one responsible in the formulation, approval, and utilization of the SEF Budget as provided under RA No. 7160 or the Local Government Code:
sustain its efforts in ensuring that all budgeted projects, programs and activities are optimally and fully implemented. This involves close monitoring on the implementation process, addressing any delays or obstacles promptly, and ensuring that allocated funds are utilized effectively to achieve desired outcomes;
from time to time, revisit the revised guidelines on the use of SEF under DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017 to stay updated on any revisions or amendments to the guidelines and ensures that SEF resources are strategically allocated according to priority programs and projects aligned with the educational needs of the locality; and
for the purpose of hiring teachers chargeable against the SEF, the LSB of the Municipality should utilize the list found in the Registry of Qualified Applicants (RQA) to ensure a transparent and merit-based selection process for hiring teachers, thus enhancing the quality of education delivery within the jurisdiction.

We recommended that management:
submit activity design, pictures and narrative reports to support the disbursements amounting to ₱216,460.48; and
comply with the proper utilization of the Special Education Fund and ensure that the programs and projects to be funded therefrom must be those aligned with Section 3 of the DepEd, DBM and DILG Joint Circular No. 1, Series 2017.

We recommended that Management direct the BAC/BAC Secretariat to prepare and submit the PMR and APCPI Results to the GPPB and comply with the posting requirements as required in R.A. No. 9184 and GPPB Circular No. 02-2020. Thereafter, please furnish the Office of the Auditor of a copy of each report.

We recommended that management implement the following measures:
Notice to Proceed:
Ensure that all Notice to Proceed documents include the date of receipt by the contractor. In cases where the date is missing, consider the date of the Notice to Proceed itself as the date of receipt.
Statement of Work Accomplished (SWA):
Implement a policy requiring the preparation of two SWAs for each project: one as of the supposed end of the contract duration and another as of the actual date of project completion. This will facilitate the accurate computation of liquidated damages against the contractor.
Preparation of SWA:
Establish a process where the SWA is prepared by the contractor and subsequently validated and/or confirmed by the procuring entity. This will ensure the accuracy and reliability of the SWA.
Certificate of Completion and Certificate of Acceptance:
Enforce the separation of the Certificate of Acceptance from the Certificate of Completion in project documentation. Additionally, ensure that the Certificate of Acceptance is issued no earlier than one year from the project's completion date.
Suspension of Work and Extension of Contract Time:
Strengthen oversight to ensure that all instances of work suspensions/extensions are supported by necessary documentary requirements. Non-compliance should result in the invalidation of the contract extension and the imposition of applicable liquidated damages in case of unjustified delays.
Non-imposition of Liquidated Damages:
Review all project contracts to ensure that liquidated damages are properly imposed for delays in project completion. Enforce the provisions of the contract agreements and the procurement law to hold contractors accountable for delays.
Submission of Procurement Documents:
Implement a system to track the submission of all procurement documents and documentary requirements on advance payment, progress payment, and final payment of infrastructure projects. Ensure that all required documents are submitted promptly to avoid delays in audit and inspection of projects.
Require personnel involved in procurement and project monitoring to undergo training and capacity-building activities to enhance their understanding of requirements and procedures.

We recommended that:
Management prepare and implement guidelines governing the grant of communication expenses to officials and employees. These guidelines should clearly outline:
Eligibility criteria for receiving communication allowance.
Allocation limits per user based on the nature of their duties and responsibilities.
Monthly ceilings/caps on communication expenses to ensure expenditures remain within budgetary constraints.
Modes of availment, including options such as postpaid plans, prepaid allocations, or cell card usage.
Procedures for obtaining approval and documentation requirements for reimbursement.
The prevailing rates and load promos offered by mobile networks should be considered.
The Municipal Accountant require personnel of National Agencies to submit a certification confirming that they had not received a similar allowance from their respective primary agencies, the same to be attached to the payroll; and
The Municipal Accountant shall ensure the completeness of supporting documents. In the case of prepaid card users, the submission of used cards should be required prior to the processing and disbursement of communication allowance, ensuring the appropriate utilization of government resources.
We recommended management to submit the lacking documentary requirements. Otherwise, we will be constrained to issue a Notice of Suspension on the above-stated transaction.

We recommended that management:
require employees to submit accomplishment reports detailing the overtime work performed;
to consider compensatory time-off as an alternative to overtime pay, in accordance with the guidelines established by CSC and DBM Joint Circular No. 2, dated October 4, 2004; and
observe the guidelines on the incurrence of engineering and administrative overhead expenses as prescribed in the yearly GAA.

We reiterated our recommendation that management reassess its practice of providing financial assistance to other LGUs, particularly those of higher income classification. Management may consider engaging in discussions with other LGUs in the Province to evaluate the practice and, if deemed necessary, discontinue it. This would help ensure that resources are utilized efficiently and effectively, in line with the principles of good governance and sound financial management.

We reiterated our recommendation and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the Acting Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to (a) reconcile their records at least quarterly to immediately detect any error/discrepancy and settle the variances noted as of end of every reporting period (b) revisit the past records to substantiate the propriety of adjustment for reconciling items; and (c) settle the dormancy of the three accounts or institute their closure, if warranted.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:
encourage the involvement of barangay officials in the tax campaign to reach out to a larger area, pursuant to Section 247 of the Local Government Code; and
implement Section 254 (a) of the Local Government Code in posting notice of delinquencies and formulate effective strategies to intensify and improve the collection of real property taxes.

We recommended and Management agreed to continue complying with DBM, DOF and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 1, dated November 4, 2020 on the implementation of PPAs to ensure that the desired socio-economic development and environmental outcomes are achieved.
We also recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the (a) Bids and Awards Committee; BAC Secretariat, and Technical Working Group to (i) work on the completion of the documentary deficiencies and submit the same to the Audit Team; (ii) enhance the transparency on procurements by complying the publicity and notification requirements; and (b) Municipal Accountant to strengthen her review function on the requisite documentation of disbursements for procurements.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:
continue its efforts to substantially comply with the guidelines on the appropriation, utilization, and accounting of LDRRMF following the rules and regulations under RA No. 10121 or the Philippine Disaster Risk Reduction Management Act of 2010 and COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012;
ensure the full implementation of the pre-disaster preparedness programs; and
require the HRM Officer to comply with the filling up of the vacant position in the LDRRMO in accordance with NDRRMC, DILG, DBM and CSC JMC No. 2014- 1 to effectively carry out the DRRM activities and programs.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the GAD Focal Point System to
create a multi-year GAD Agenda as basis for the annual GAD Plan and Budget by using the templates provided in PCW Memorandum Circular No. 2018-04 dated September 19, 2018; and
(b) 	timely submit to the Audit Team the GAD Accomplishment Report in compliance with COA Circular No. 2014-001.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:
Instruct the BPLO to:
strictly enforce the Revenue Code of Quezon, Nueva Ecija;
efficiently utilize reports from the Accounting Office for assessing business tax on entities with transactions in the Municipality; and
mandate all business taxpayers to submit a certified copy of their Income Tax Return (ITR).
Direct the Municipal Treasurer to examine and verify the books of accounts and pertinent records for business establishments with unreliable declared sales amounts to ensure accuracy.
Task both the BPLO and the Municipal Treasurer to:
Verify, assess, and validate the identified under-collection deficiencies and subsequently demand the collection thereof, including the disclosed under-collection of business taxes from six business establishments totaling ₱32,681.83; and
Require the registration of the identified businesses that were not included in the list of registered business within the Municipality.

We recommended and Management agreed to strictly observe the regular and intact remittance of the total amount of taxes withheld from employees’ compensation and from payment of goods and services to assure that the government was not deprived of taxes that can be used in the implementation of various programs and projects.

We recommended and Management agreed to continue complying with the GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG regulations on the withholding and remittance of employees’ and employer’ contributions and loan amortizations of employees to the respective agencies.

Business Partner (BP) Number with the GSIS System, to the HRD for the preparation
of the ARA, and immediately remit the same to the GSIS.

b. Refund the balance to concerned employees for the incorrect deductions.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Identify the breakdown and establish the validity of the unreconciled balances of
the Due to PhilHealth and Due to Pag-lBIG accounts.

b. Afterwards, make the necessary adjustments or remit to the PhilHealth and HDMF
the established balances after reconciliation.

c. Henceforth, ensure the proper monitoring and recording of contributions withheld
and remitted in the said accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MT to monitor the maintenance
of the CO's cashbook conforming to Items a, b, and c of Section 181 of GAAM, Volume
I.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entry in the books of the
Municipality for CY 2023 by debiting cash and crediting the appropriate
income/liability/equity accounts totaling P93,680.09; and
b. Municipal Treasurer and the RCOs to remit daily collections at the close of
each business day.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the MSWDO to ensure the timely
liquidation of the funds received from the National Government Agencies and
comply with the provisions of the Memorandum of Agreement.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Submit all DVs for cash advance of confidential funds together with its supporting
documents within seven working days from the release of the check to the Audit Team
in a sealed envelope with a visible label “CONFIDENTIAL – For the COA ATL Only” and
with a covering transmittal letter enumerating the submissions;
b. Clearly state the duration of implementation of the projects in the DV;
c. Ensure the certification that the previous cash advance was liquidated and the PNP
certification for the need to release the fund are dated and provided before the
grant/release of the cash advance;
d. Religiously follow the Physical and Financial Plan for confidential/intelligence fund;
e. Ensure that all cash advances for CF and IF are liquidated within 30 days after every
quarter, or from the approved target date of completion of the project/activity, or after
the cash advance has been fully utilized whichever comes first.
Henceforth, ensure to comply with the provisions of COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND
Joint Circular No. 2015-01.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
• Direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare year-end adjusting entries to revert
the unreleased checks totaling P4,363,442.54 to the cash in bank account and
recognize the corresponding liability/payable account;
• Direct the Municipal Treasurer to comply with the procedures in MNGAS for
LGUs pertaining to reporting of disbursements/checks issued; and
• Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that recording of check
disbursements will only be done after receipt of RCI with DV and supporting
documents from the Municipal Treasurer, to avoid inclusion of unreleased
checks as reconciling items in the Bank Reconciliation Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct:
• The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to coordinate with the
bank for possible identification of the source of the online deposit in the amount
of P389.82 and upon confirming with the bank, secure the supporting
documents to ensure validity of the transactions and for its proper recording in
the books of accounts maintained by the Municipal Accountant, and in the
cashbooks maintained by the Municipal Treasurer; and
• The Municipal Accountant to record the check reorder fee amounting to
P15,000.00 upon securing the supporting document from the Municipal
Treasurer.

Henceforth, ensure that all valid reconciling items reflected in the BRS were properly
supported with documents.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
prepare the following adjusting entry in the respective books of accounts:

General Fund:
To record the reclassification of Accounts Payable
Accounts Payable 2,009,730.47
Other Payables 26,485.37
Due to Officers and Employees 1,983,245.10

Special Education Fund:
To record the reclassification of Accounts Payable
Accounts Payable 20,000.00
Other Payables 20,000.00

Also, instruct the Municipal Accountant to submit to the Audit Team the supporting
documents for the above-mentioned claims/transactions for further validation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
prepare and maintain a special account for 20% Development Fund and render the
required records/reports for that special account separately under the General
Fund in compliance with the provisions of R.A. No. 7160 and Chapter 6 of MNGAS
for LGUs, Volume I and to facilitate monitoring of the utilization of the fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to record
unpaid terminal leave benefits as at year-end in the Leave Benefits Payable account
instead of AP account and correct the error in recording terminal leave benefits as AP.
Moreover, furnish the Audit Team copy of the adjusting entries made for validation

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Make the adjusting entry by debiting the CIB – LCCA and crediting the AP
accounts both by the amount of checks still unreleased; and
b. Strictly comply with Section 43 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I by ensuring that
only checks released to the claimants are recorded in the Check Disbursements
to avoid understating both the CIB-LCCA and the AP accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the AOs to ensure that all CAs
pertaining to the salaries and wages of JOs and COs are liquidated within five days
from the date of each CA in compliance with COA Circular No. 97-002.

We reiterated our previous years’ recommendations that the Municipal Mayor:
• Require the MGSO and the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with each other
and complete the reconciliation of the affected PPE accounts to establish the
correct PPE balances, and to investigate and make necessary adjustments in
their respective records, as necessary;
• Instruct the MGSO to prepare an updated IIRUP and submit a copy to the Audit
Team; and
• Enjoin the Asset Disposal Committee to cause the immediate disposal of the
unserviceable properties, in accordance with existing rules and regulations, to
avoid further deterioration of the assets and generate additional income for the
Municipality.

Additionally, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
• Review and revise, if necessary, the composition of the Inventory Committees,
and facilitate the conduct of One-time cleansing of PPE accounts, in
accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006; and
• Adopt internal strategies that shall cause the periodic reconciliation between
accounting and GSO records to ensure the accuracy of the PPE balances, in
accordance with Section 114 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the Municipal
Accountant to draw the necessary adjusting entries to correct the Accounts
Payable, Due to Officer and Employees, Due to GOCCs and Other Payable account
balances, if still unpaid.

Henceforth, ensure that liabilities are recorded in their proper accounts, guided by
COA Circular No. 2015-009.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct the Municipal
Accountant and the MGSO to:
• Complete the reconciliation of the accounting with property records to
establish correct balances for the inventories; and
• Investigate and adjust any discrepancies noted.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the Municipal
Accountant to:
• Draw the necessary adjusting entries to correct the balance of CIP accounts;
• Implement internal mechanisms that shall ensure the timely reclassification of
CIP to appropriate asset accounts upon completion, pursuant to Section 50 of
MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I; and
• Ensure that Depreciation expenses are properly recognized in accordance with
PAG 3 of IPSAS 17.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
• Enjoin the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) to avoid
including PPAs with potential issues, especially on ROW, under the 20%
Development Fund;
• Direct the MPDC to indicate the target implementation schedule of PPAs under
the 20% Development Fund, to increase organizational coordination,
effectiveness and efficiency; and
• Ensure that PPAs funded by the 20% Development Fund are well-planned and
procurement-and-implementation ready, in accordance with Item 3.2.3 of DBM-
DOF-DILG JMC No. 1, s. 2020.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
• Ensure that a more robust policy on the payment of JO wages is developed
and strictly implemented, to enable the timely CA liquidation of disbursing
officers and avoid the incurrence of overlapping CAs;
• Instruct the disbursing officers to ensure that CAs are liquidated within the
period prescribed by COA Circular NO. 97-002;
• Direct the Municipal Accountant to adopt appropriate strategies to ensure that
new CAs are not granted to officials with unliquidated CAs; and
• Consider the payment of JO wages via ATMs, to decrease, if not eliminate, the
need for multiple CAs to be drawn by disbursing officers.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
• Submit the supporting documents showing that goods and services were
received and accepted as of December 31, 2023, to justify inclusion in the
Accounts Payable balance;
• Draw the necessary adjusting entries to revert Accounts Payable totaling
P2,250,336.90 to the Unappropriated Surplus of the General Fund;
• Adopt appropriate strategies and internal mechanisms that would facilitate the
timely completion of documents by end-users and their submission to the
Accounting Office for processing the payment of Accounts Payable and a
more effective monitoring of Accounts Payable, to minimize, if not eliminate,
the existence of Accounts Payable outstanding for more than two years; and
• Ensure that the JEVs prepared to record year-end Accounts Payable are
supported with documents evidencing the receipt and acceptance of goods and
services.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the:
• MDRRMO to include the list of PPAs charged to STF in LDRRMFIP under a
separate caption, in compliance with Item 5.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-002;
• MDRRMO to indicate a more specific implementation schedule in LDRRMFIP;
• Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer to adopt appropriate
strategies that would minimize, if not eliminate, the need for reconciliation of
budget and accounting records that may affect the timeliness of the
preparation of monthly utilization reports; and
Henceforth, ensure that the monthly utilization reports are submitted within 15
days of the ensuing month, in accordance with Item 5.1.5 of COA Circular No.
2012-002.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a) Direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare adjusting entries to revert the
unreleased checks totaling P2,724,429.57 to the cash in bank account and
recognize the corresponding liability/payable account;
b) Direct the Municipal Treasurer to ensure that only those checks released to
claimants shall be reported in the Report of Checks Issued in compliance with
Section 43 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I; and
c) Develop and implement internal control measures to enable all unreleased
checks in the custody of the Office of the Municipal Treasurer to be
communicated to the Office of the Municipal Accountant, to avoid inclusion of
unreleased checks as reconciling items in the Bank Reconciliation Statements.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Planning and Development Coordinator to ensure that all development projects to
be funded out of the 20% Development Fund are well-planned and procurement-
and-implementation ready.
Henceforth, comply with guidelines set in the DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 in the
appropriation and utilization of the 20% Development Fund of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
• Municipal DRRM Officer to prepare and submit the monthly Report on the Sources

• Municipal Accountant to certify correct the monthly report prepared by the DRRM
Officer before submission of the same to the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and
the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the balances of the individual TF bank

We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant make the necessary
corrective actions or adjustments after reconciliation to reflect the correct
individual account balances in the books of accounts, including the erroneously
recorded purchase of office supplies and food packs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Make the necessary adjustments to the Due to Officers and Employees, Due to
LGUs, Due to GOCCs, Due to Pag-IBIG, and Due to NGAs accounts amounting to
P602,991.99, P277,300.10, P14,910.00, P256,953.57, and P3,125.91, respectively
regarding the aforementioned claims if upon verification, there are still
unpaid/unsettled.
b. Provide the Audit Team with the schedule of the AP account together with its
complete details/breakdown; and
c. Submit to the Audit Team the POs/contracts and proof of delivery of the payables
without presented supporting documents.

Also, ensure that the recorded payables are supported with valid and sufficient
evidence regarding the claim/obligation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Make the necessary steps to strengthen the Municipality's internal control and
require the Municipal Accountant to avoid the occurrence of abnormal balances
in the books of accounts and Notes to FS and to always ensure the accuracy and
reliability of accounting data; and
b. Require the Municipal Accountant to review the entries made which affect the
erroneously recorded transactions and make the necessary adjusting entries in
the books of accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Inventory Committee to:

b. Ensure that all PPE balances are reported properly in the RPCPPE;
c. Ensure that the RPCPPE for each year is submitted to the Audit Team on or
before January 31 of the following year; and
d. Conduct the one-time cleansing in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006.

Also, we further recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Inventory
Committee and the Municipal Accountant to ensure that accounting records and
the RPCPPE are reconciled.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to ensure
that all:
a. Collections are remitted to the liquidating officer before the close of every

b. Collections are deposited intact;
c. RCDs are supported with VDSs; and
d. RCDs are certified by the collector and acknowledged by the liquidating officer.

Also, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to
submit to the Audit Team the VDSs totaling P3,143,965.37.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare a year-end adjusting entries to revert

b. direct the Municipal Treasurer to comply with the procedures in MNGAS for LGUs

c. require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that recording of check
disbursements will only be done after receipt of RCI with DV and supporting

as reconciling items in the BRS.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to

Accounts Payable 1,700,288.68
Leave Benefit Payable 1,425,406.60
Other Payable 274,882.08

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor consider undergoing the One-Time
Cleansing of PPE account balances as prescribed by COA Circular No. 2020-006 to

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
clearly state in the DV for cash advances for Confidential and/or Intelligence Fund the duration of

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to request for the authority for the write-off of the dormant and unaccounted balances in the Due to NGAs account with the Commission on Audit.

Municipal Accountant to ensure that:

b. a certification that the previous cash advance has been liquidated and accounted for in the books is attached to the DVs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare an adjusting entry to reclassify the 20 percent share of the Municipality in the proceeds collected in CYs 2018-2021 from Fire Code Fees totaling P123,683.63 from the Due to NGAs account to the Trust Liabilities account.

Henceforth, ensure that all recorded transactions in the Due to NGAs account meet the definition criteria as prescribed by the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. MT to liquidate the CAs for the salaries and wages of JO Personnel within five days after each 15 day/end of the month pay period; and
b. MA to attach certification that the previous CA has been liquidated and accounted for in the books before processing of DV on the grant of the new CA.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to immediately submit the SORDs of the mentioned projects to the DSWD and return unused balance, if any.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the MT to:

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to:

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to commence and undergo the One-Time cleansing of PPE account balances as prescribed by COA Circular No. 2020-006 to come up with reliable PPE balances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Officer (MPDO) to ensure that all development projects to be funded out of the 20% Development Fund are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation ready.

Henceforth, comply with guidelines set in the DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 in the appropriation and utilization of the 20% Development Fund of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Accountable Officers to ensure that all cash advances pertaining to the salaries and wages of JOs, and COS are liquidated within five (5) days from the date of each cash advance in compliance with COA Circular No. 97-002.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
make the adjusting entry in the books of accounts to reclassify the

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant or his authorized representative to submit all the DVs for the

Team Leader within seven days from release of the check, in accordance with Item
6.1.8.4 of COA-DBM-DILG-DND-GCG Joint Circular No. 2015-01.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
• Direct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to comply with the
procedures in MNGAS for LGUs pertaining to reporting of disbursements/checks
issued; and
• Develop and implement internal control measures to enable all undelivered
checks in the custody of MTO to be communicated to the Office of the Municipal
Accountant.
Henceforth, ensure that undelivered checks as of year-end are reported as part of the
Cash in bank account in compliance with the existing rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
• Acting GSO and the Municipal Accountant to include in their records the parcel of land
and the motor vehicle at unit costs/values as prescribed on Item 6.2.12 of COA Circular
2020-006;
• Acting GSO to exert effort in the verification of ownership on the six parcels of land
located in Barangay Quipot, Tiaong, Quezon; and
• Inventory Committee to tag all counted PPE items with new property stickers
containing the Property Number and all vital information on the PPE item as provided
under Paragraph 5.7 of COA Circular No. 2020-006.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
➢ Municipal Accountant to:
• Maintain subsidiary ledgers for transfers of the Municipality’s unutilized
DRRMF to the STF by year of transfer; and
• Disclose in the Notes to the Financial Statements the available balance of STF as
well as its utilization and residual balance per year of transfer as exhibited in Annex
D of COA Circular No. 2012-002; and
➢ Municipal Accountant and the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer
to reconcile their records on which year the unutilized balance of DRRMF in the
amount of P19,479,176.42 pertains.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Accountable Officers to ensure
that all cash advances pertaining to the salaries and wages of JOs and COS are liquidated
within five days after the pay period in compliance with COA Circular No. 97-002.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor revisit the possibility and necessity
of payment of salaries and wages of JOs and COS through Automated Teller Machine as
internal control to prevent delayed liquidation or unliquidated cash advances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Acting GSO to:
• Expedite the application of the insurable properties with the GSIS;
• Prepare and submit the Property Inventory Form to the Supervising Auditor/Audit
Team Leader, and the General Insurance Fund, GSIS, not later than April 30 of
each year; and
• Facilitate the proper procedures in transferring the ownership of properties to its
end-users including the cancellation of issued Acknowledgement Receipt for
Equipment to the Barangay pertaining to motor vehicle and furnish the Municipal
Accountant all the necessary documents supporting such transfers.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
prepare the necessary accounting entries relating to transfers and recognition of
properties upon receipt of pertinent documents, if warranted.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to maintain
a Subsidiary Ledger for each collecting officer pursuant to the provisions set forth in Item

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

a. Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to prepare the SORD for the purchase
