Cards/Stock Cards and always reconcile the property records with the records
of the accounting;
 Instruct the OIC-PGSO to verify the existence of property items/PPE accounts
that were recorded in the books of accounts but not included in the inventory
report submitted and include the same in the RPCPPE after the conduct of the
physical inventory;
 Direct the OIC-Provincial Accountant to analyze reconciling items such as the
small tangible items that are included in the books of accounts as PPE, and
accordingly, prepare adjusting journal entries to reflect the correct balances of
75
the affected accounts; and
 Require the OIC-PGSO to renew the PAR every January of the third year after its
issuance or whenever there is a change in custodianship to ensure the
accountability of the property issued and for proper monitoring of PPE.

We recommended that the Provincial Governor require the OIC-PGSO to submit the
RIS and the List of Recipients to the Provincial Accounting Office who will adjust
the corresponding accounts accordingly.

We recommended that the Provincial Governor require the OIC-Provincial
Accountant to:
a) Record the completed projects identified as dormant for two to 15 years with a
total amount of P51,783,596.73 (P52,659,798.78 less P876,202.05 that should be
recorded as repairs and maintenance) to the corresponding Property, Plant
and Equipment (PPE) account after securing the documents from the
Provincial Engineering Office;
b) Compute for the related depreciation of the respective PPE accounts and
prepare the necessary Journal Entry Voucher to record the same;
c) Analyze the transaction that was set-up as accounts payable and if found
already paid and/or not a valid claim, make an adjusting entry; and
d) Adjust the amount of P876,202.05 to Prior Period Adjustment to record the
cost of repairs and maintenance incurred in CYs 2014, 2015, 2017 and 2019.

We recommended that the Provincial Governor instruct the OIC-Provincial
Accountant to:
a) Reconcile the record of liquidations between the books of the Province and
the books of the Municipalities;
b) Perform periodic verification, analysis and validation of the Due from/ Due to
LGUs accounts with concerned LGUs; and
c) Draw necessary journal entries to reflect the correct/adjusted account
balances.

We recommended that the Provincial Governor require the OIC-Provincial
Accountant to: a) maintain sufficient records/details of trust funds and regularly
monitor its use to ensure the implementation of the projects for which it was
created; b) reconcile the unliquidated fund transfers by sending confirmation
letters to the source agencies and return the unexpended balances, if warranted;
and c) consider to adopt COA circular relative to write-off pursuant to COA Circular
No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and the Sangguniang
Panlalawigan ensure the timely passage of the succeeding appropriation
ordinances in accordance with the provisions of Republic Act No. 7160 to enable
the full and immediate implementation of PPAs devoted to pandemic response
under the different services of the Province to the advantage of the community.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and the Sangguniang
Panlalawigan ensure that the succeeding appropriation ordinances are timely
passed to enable the prompt execution of PPAs funded under the 20 percent
Development Fund so as not to deprive the intended beneficiaries of the desired
benefits and services that could be derived from the socio-economic development
and environmental outcomes.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant  exert  more  efforts  in
reconstructing the SLs of the former Municipal Treasurer pertaining  to  her
cash accountability depending on the availability  of  reliable  records  in
accordance with Sections 111(1) and 114(2) of PD No.  1445  and  submit  the
same to the Audit Team for verification and audit.

      We also  recommended  that  the  Management  demand  from  the  former
Municipal Treasurer to a) produce cashbook for SEF;  b)  reconcile  all  her
cashbooks with accounting records; and c) thereafter  submit  to  the  Audit
Team the report of her accountability and settle the  remaining  balance  of
such accountability pursuant to Section 80 of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that the:


   a. Municipal Mayor (i) require the Acting  Municipal  Treasurer  and  the
      Municipal Accountant to immediately  reconcile  their  records  as  at
      December 31, 2021 and submit a reconciliation  statement  thereon  and
      take-up the necessary adjusting entries in their  respective  records;
      (ii) demand from/require the  Acting  Municipal  Treasurer  and  other
      accountable officers (AOs) concerned  to  immediately  submit  to  the
      Municipal  Accountant  the  source   documents   of   the   unrecorded
      disbursements and deposits, and other book  reconciling  items,  after
      which, the same shall  be  submitted  to  the  Audit  Team  for  audit
      purposes; and (iii) make representations with the bank to furnish  the
      Municipality with copies of debit and credit memos of said reconciling
      items for recording in the books of accounts;


   b. Acting Municipal Treasurer (i)  strictly  observe  the  provisions  of
      Section 63(F) of the LTOM on the reconciliation of  cashbook  balances
      with subsidiary accounting records as this  is  an  important  control
      tool for cash; and (ii) monitor long outstanding checks on  a  regular
      basis and notify the payees concerned to return the stale  checks  for
      the issuance of replacement checks in accordance with Section 83(b)(4)
      of the LTOM; and

   c. Municipal Accountant to further investigate and  provide  details  for
      all reconciling items in the BRS and  thereafter  make  the  necessary
      adjustments in the books.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Municipal Mayor  (a) thru, the
Municipal Accountant, regularly send written notice to AOs to demand
settlement of their outstanding cash advances; (b) consider withholding  the
salaries of AOs still connected with the Municipality who refuse  to  settle
their accounts without justifiable reason; and (c) institute  the  necessary
legal action against those  who  failed  to  settle  their  accountabilities
despite demand, if warranted.

      The  Municipal  Accountant  exert  efforts  to  locate  documents  and
previous records to facilitate reconciliation and/or  determination  of  the
causes of the negative cash  advance  balances  and  thereafter  effect  the
necessary adjustment in the books of accounts, as appropriate.

      In all instances, the Municipal Mayor  and  the  Municipal  Accountant
must ensure that the provisions of COA  Circular  No.  97-002  are  strictly
observed in the granting and liquidation of cash advances and see  that  all
cash advances are fully liquidated at the end of the year.

      We also advised the Municipal Mayor to file a request for authority to
write-off dormant unliquidated cash advances to this office pursuant to  the
provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor make  written  representations
with the management of the IA  to  inform  them  of  the  unliquidated  fund
transfers for the reconciliation of their  records  by  requesting  them  to
submit  reports  and  other  necessary   documents   supporting   the   fund
utilization and return the unexpended  balances,  if  any.  Thereafter,  the
Municipal  Accountant  should  record  the  liquidation  in  the  books   of
accounts.

      We also recommended that the Municipality regularly monitor  the  IA’s
compliance with the terms and conditions of the MOA to ensure that the  fund
transfers are utilized according to its intended purpose.

We recommended that the Inventory Committee expidite  the  conduct  of
inventory-taking activities,  reconciliation  with  pertinent  records,  and
preparation and submission of reports for  the  one-time  cleansing  of  PPE
accounts in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2020-006.

      We also recommended that  the  Municipal  Accountant  and  the  Acting
General Services Officer (GSO) ensure that proper accounting  and  reporting
procedures  for  all  acquisition/receipt/issue/transfer/disposal   of   PPE
pursuant to the relevant laws, rules and regulations on property and  supply
management are  strictly  followed.  The  Municipal  Mayor  should  likewise
ensure that physical inventory is undertaken annually  and  reports  thereon
are submitted accordingly.

We recommended that the Inventory Committee expedite  the  preparation
and  submission  of  the  RPCLRN  to  the  Municipal  Accountant  who  shall
reconcile the local road inventory report and road map with  those  recorded
in the RPI and prepare the journal  entry  voucher  to  recognize  the  Road
Networks account  in  the  books  of  accounts,  in  coordination  with  the
Municipal Engineer and the Acting GSO. Thereafter, the Municipal  Accountant
and the Acting GSO should maintain the  relevant  records  required  by  COA
Circular No. 2015-008.
      The Municipal Mayor ensure that  the  physical  count  of  local  road
networks is undertaken annually and the reports  thereon  are  submitted  to
the Audit Team and the Municipal Accountant not later  than  January  31  of
each year.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer
and the MPDC to submit to the Municipal Accountant  the  list  of  completed
projects, supported with Certificate of Completion and Certificate of  Final
Acceptance, to serve as basis in the  adjustment  of  the  CIP  and  related
asset account balances. Moreover, the Municipal  Engineer  should  regularly
monitor on-going projects and immediately submit the necessary  reports  and
documents to the Municipal Accountant to support the transfer  of  completed
projects to the appropriate asset account.

We recommended that the Municipal  Accountant,  in  pursuance  of  the
provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005, (i) conduct  regular  and  periodic
verification, analysis, and validation of the existence  of  fund  transfers
per source agency concerned; (ii) reconcile the balances between the  source
and implementing government entities concerned and effect adjustment in  the
books by providing separate SL per agency and purpose of the  fund  transfer
in compliance with Section 111(1)  of  PD  No.  1445,  and  segregating  the
unsupported, undocumented and unidentified balances; and (iii)  ensure  that
the the balances reported in the SLs are consistent with the details in  the
Notes to Financial Statements. The Municipal Accountant should also  prepare
aging of dormant  inter-agency  fund  transfers  on  a  quarterly  basis  to
support the request for write-off.

      Thereafter, the Municipal Mayor is  advised  to  file  a  request  for
authority from the COA, through the Audit Team, to  write-off  dormant  fund
transfers  supported  with  the  required  documents,  to   facilitate   the
cleansing of the  books  for  the  fair  presentation  of  accounts  in  the
financial statements.

We  recommended  that  the  Municipal  Mayor,   thru   the   Municipal
Accountant, (i) cause the immediate submission  of  the  aforementioned
lacking documents; and (ii) ensure strict compliance with the provisions of
COA Circular No. 2012-001 on common documentary requirements  and  DBM
Budget Circular No. 2004-5A before processing and certifying the DVs/payrolls
for the  grant  of  honoraria.

      We also recommended that the BAC members, TWG chair and  members,  and
BAC Secretariat  members  return  the  excess  honoraria  received  by  them
totaling ₱340,640.00 and submit certified copies of ORs to  the  Audit  Team
for verification. Otherwise, the same shall be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that Management:

   a. Control and evaluate the use of  fuel  by  requiring  presentation  of
      properly accomplished and approved trip tickets prior to  approval  of
      requests for fuel/oil;

   b. Require the officials concerned to properly and  completely  fill  out
      the trip tickets and adhere strictly to the guidelines promulgated  in
      the use of government motor vehicles; and

   c. Submit to the Audit Team  the  monthly  Report  of  Official  Travels,
      Report  of  Fuel  Consumption  and  trip  tickets  pertaining  to  the
      aforementioned  transactions,  and  a  complete  list  of  serviceable
      municipal vehicles for validation purposes.

We recommended that the Municipality  observe  economy  and  austerity
measures in the use of government funds, especially in the serving of  meals
and snacks to municipal officials and employees  during  meetings,  sessions
and other activities  which  are  part  of  the  normal  operations  of  the
offices/committees concerned. Instead, focus the utilization  of  government
funds for purposes which are more beneficial to the constituents to  prevent
the incurrence of unnecessary expenses.

We recommended that the Management, thru the Municipal Human Resources
Office, prioritize and consider filling-out the vacant positions by hiring
new employees or expedite promotion of deserving employees to enhance its
services to attain effective, efficient and full  implementation  of  PPAs
of the Municipality for the optimal benefit of its constituents as well as
to provide equal job opportunities to the community.

Further,  personnel  movement  has  to  be  effected  in   order   to
acknowledge the exemplary performance of employees by way  of  promotion
to higher rank thereby allowing the entry of new applicants for lower
level positions.

We recommended to the management to require the Municipal Accountant to effect adjusting journal entries to record the said remaining inventories at year-end. Record the receipt and issuance of all supplies using the perpetual inventory method, considering the proper accounts provided in COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015. Moreover, stop the improper practice of attaching Requisition Issue Slip (RIS) to disbursement vouchers as basis of expending inventories outrightly.

 Further, required the General Services Office and Municipal Health Office to provide the Municipal Accounting Office a Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) supported with the prescribed format of RIS for already issued supplies and drugs and medicines during the period, either weekly or monthly whichever is more convenient, as basis for the latter to prepare Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to record the already expended/issued supplies.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accounting Office (MAO) effected necessary adjusting entries. Started using the perpetual inventory method in recording supplies and created and inventory committee that conducted physical count of assets.

observations.recommendation: We recommended to the management to require the Municipal Engineer to submit pertinent documents to the Municipal Accountant as her basis to draw Journal Entry Voucher (JEV), along with its working paper, to drop the demolished infrastructures from the books of accounts of the agency. Likewise, required the Municipal Accountant to draw JEV, along with its working papers and supporting documents, to adjust the recorded equipment which failed to meet the P15,000.00 capitalization threshold. Submit to this audit team copies thereof for our evaluation.
observations.managementAction: The MAO made journal entries to drop the demolished infrastructures from the books of accounts of the agency and abided with the other audit recommendations.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to the management to require the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer and BAC Chairman, to submit the noted lacking supporting documents to the audit team for post-audit, in order to conclude the appropriateness, legality and validity of the disbursed transactions. 

Moreover, required the Municipal Accountant to, henceforth, meticulously and sufficiently support disbursement vouchers with required pertinent documents prior to payment and submission to audit team.
observations.managementAction: The management submitted the lacking documents noted in this audit finding.
The management complied with the audit recommendation and submitted the lacking documents noted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended to the Local Chief Executive to be prudent in authorizing the disbursements from the discretionary fund and require the Municipal Accountant to establish first the legal and specific purpose of the payments by attaching the required documentations and other supporting documents as are necessary depending on the nature of expense charged.
observations.managementAction: The management submitted the lacking documents needed to establish the legality of the related disbursements.

observations.recommendation: We recommended to the management of LGU Quezon to exercise prudence and good sense in the disbursement of government funds. Henceforth, stop the grant of mortuary financial assistance to Quezon (Bukidnon) Municipal Government Employees Union (QMGEU) which is not authorized by existing laws, rules and regulations.
observations.managementAction: The management has stopped the grant of mortuary financial assistance to QMGEU.

A validation team was created to verify the eligibility of social amelioration program beneficiaries per Memorandum issued last February 22, 2021 and a committee who will work in close coordination with barangay officials concerned. Moreover, the MSWDO submitted to audit team the required lacking documents.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to the Local Chief Executive to require the concerned departments to submit the original distribution sheets/lists to the audit team. Ensure that such documents are duly signed by the recipients to prove their actual receipt of relief goods and duly signed by the municipal officials and staff who facilitated the actual distribution. Prior to submission, ensure also that the total quantity of the procured relief goods is reconciled with the total quantity upon distribution, so as not to warrant issuance of disallowance on discrepancies noted, if there is any. 

Moreover, required the Municipal Accounting Office (MAO) to account, henceforth, the procured goods as inventories and not as outright expenses. Refrain from attaching RIS prior to actual release or distribution. Likewise, the MAO and the General Services Office (GSO) to maintain Supplies Ledger Cards and Stock Cards, respectively, to establish good internal control in recording and monitoring the movement of all inventories procured.
observations.managementAction: The management submitted to the audit team the lacking distribution sheets/lists and adopted appropriate measures as recommended.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to the management to submit valid justification/s as to why the identified procured medical equipment charged against the Bayanihan Grant are not yet placed in a location and condition necessary for its operation, which are supposed-to-be intended for COVID-19 emergency response. Likewise, provide action plan on how to resolve this issue.

Further, required the Municipal Accountant to draw Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to adjust the improperly recognized depreciation expenses totaling P235,525.00 supported with working papers and subsequently provide this audit team a copy thereof.
observations.managementAction: Justification has been transmitted to the audit team. A corresponding JEV was drawn to adjust the improperly recognized depreciation expenses.

observations.recommendation: We recommended to direct the Municipal Accountant to reissue demand letters and exhaust all possible remedies to collect the receivables and to demand liquidation of cash advances and fund transfers. Conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of these accounts. Comply with all the conditions and requirements of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 for the possible request for the write off of the specified dormant receivables and cash advances accounts to the Commission on Audit. Provide allowance for impairment thereafter and reconcile the accounts with unidentified/ unreconciled subsidiary ledgers from prior years in order to have a fair presentation of affected accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The management reissued demand letter to accountable Officers for the liquidation of the accounts. Ongoing reconciliation and analysis of the unidentified accounts. Request for write off will be made after reconciliation of receivable accounts.
observations.reason: Non-settlement of receivables due to unwillingness and resignation of the accountable officers.

observations.recommendation: We recommended management to require the submission of the written conformity and acquiescence of the Solicitor General or the Government Corporate Counsel, as the case maybe, and the written concurrence of the Commission on Audit shall first be secured before the hiring or employment of a private lawyer or law firm.
observations.managementAction: The management adhered to the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to require the Municipal Accountant to continue the reconciliation of land reported in lump amount and disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements necessary information on these real properties.

Require the Municipal Assessor to constantly follow up the status of the submitted documents (for titling of the real properties of the municipality at the Registry of Deeds and DENR provincial office at Malaybalay City to ensure titling of all the lands owned by the municipality in compliance to Section 47 (C) of the Implementing Rules and Regulations of the Local Government Code of 1991 and Section 148 of COA Circular No. 92-386 dated October 20, 1992.   

Direct the General Services Officer to keep a complete record of all real properties owned by the LGU in compliance to Articles 122 and 440 of the IRR of RA 7160.
observations.managementAction: Process the entitlement of land owned by the municipality.
observations.reason: Still waiting for the issuance of title of deeds.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to require the Local Chief Executive to create an Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of all local roads and prepare Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System. 

Direct the GSO to maintain a Local Road Inventory, Road Map, Local Road Network Property Card, and prepare Report on Local Road Network for all roads and its components annually. Then, instruct the Municipal Engineer to render complete description and cost segregation of all road components, serving as basis for the Municipal Accountant to prepare Journal Voucher to record the costing of local road network in the general ledger and in the Local Road Network Ledger Card.
observations.managementAction: Reconstituted Inventory Committee to conduct physical count. To prepare/render report and other documents.
observations.reason: Neglected by management in CY 2021.  The Municipal Engineer failed to render cost segregation of all road components serving as basis for the Municipal Accountant to record the costing of local road networks. The GSO has not maintained the Local Road Inventory, Road Map, Local Road Network Property Card and the Report on Local Road Network for all roads and its components. Inventory Committee needs training to handle the task.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
Coordinate with the Authorized Government Depository Banks (AGDB) to obtain the corresponding documents related to the reconciling items and prepare adjusting entries to record the transactions;
Establish the existence of the seven bank accounts by coordinating with AGDB and immediately prepare and reconcile the BRS for all the depository accounts of the City; and
Henceforth, strictly comply with COA Circular No. 96-011 relative to BRS of the City.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the HCDRD to strictly monitor the implementation of the provisions outlined in each Contract Agreement for Bistekville Housing Projects and submit complete documentation of such to CAD for the propriety of recording the transactions in the books;
Reconcile the receivable accounts of HCDRD and CAD to ensure accuracy, completeness and propriety of amounts recorded and effect the necessary adjusting entries; and
Henceforth, strictly comply with the provisions of Paragraph 7 of 
IPSAS 1 and Annex B of COA Circular 2015-009.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the HCDRD to submit complete and accurate documentation for each Bistekville Project to CAD for the propriety of recording the transactions in the books;
Require the HCDRD and CAD to reconcile their respective receivable accounts to ensure accuracy, completeness and propriety of amounts reported and effect the necessary adjusting entries in the books of the City; and
Henceforth, strictly comply with the provisions of Paragraph 27 of IPSAS 1 and Annex A of COA Circular 2015-010.

We recommended that Management:
Require all concerned AOs to settle all outstanding cash advances immediately by demanding the submission of the liquidation reports and their supporting documents;
Direct the City Legal Officer to institute immediate appropriate action and/or legal remedies, if necessary, for AOs who refused to liquidate their outstanding cash advances; and
Henceforth, see that all outstanding cash advances are liquidated as soon as the purpose for which it was granted has already been served.

We recommended that Management:
Issue a demand letter immediately to PPI to submit the reports showing the successful crediting of the amounts to the beneficiaries totaling P73,905,000.00;
Immediately stop the practice of processing payments without complete supporting documents and submit the lacking documents to COA for verification and audit; and
Strictly comply with the provisions of the PSDA and Sections 4(6) of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that the CAD:
Coordinate with the GSD, other concerned departments, and end-users on the reconciliation of the Inventory account balances;
Conduct thorough analysis/review of the inventory accounts and make the necessary adjusting entries to record the drugs and medicines, non-accountable forms and office supplies expenses; and
Strictly adhere to the accrual accounting requirements under Paragraphs 1 and 7 of IPSAS 1.

We recommended that:
Management –
Require the concerned implementing offices/departments to evaluate further the various PPAs funded by the NGAs for subsequent implementation so that the intended beneficiaries can enjoy and offer benefits and services;
Make representations with the SAs for the approval of transfer of the said balances to the City GF, or return the same to the SAs if it is expressly provided for under the MOA, if already deemed unimplementable and allowed in the provisions of the MOA.
The CAD –
Locate immediately and establish the documents supporting the non-moving balances for proper monitoring and recording in the books;
Provide copies of the MOA or SAROs covering the transfers of the said funds to establish the information needed to evaluate the abovementioned funds and implement the same, if still necessary; and
Reconcile their records with the SAs, and if necessary, make adjustments to establish the correct balance of the accounts in the financial statement.

We recommended that Management:
Stop the practice of entering into a contract for services with a duration beyond the budget year and align the procurement with the APP; and
Henceforth, regularly conduct the EPAs to ensure that expenditures are within the budget year and supported by an appropriation.

We recommended that the Management:
Immediately submit the lacking supporting documentation to the Audit Team;
Ensure that all succeeding program implementations of the City are consistently supported with complete documentation; and
Henceforth, strictly comply with Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 and DSWD Memorandum Circular No. 04, s. 2020.

We recommended that the Management:
Immediately submit the lacking supporting documentation to the Audit Team;
Ensure that all succeeding implementations of the SPISC Program of the City are consistently supported with complete documentation; and
Henceforth, strictly comply with Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445, DSWD Memorandum Circular No. 04, s. 2019 and 2020.

We recommended that Management, moving forward:
Require the QCDRRM Office to coordinate with other offices/departments in the preparation of the LDRRMFIP to avoid duplication of projects that may lead to funds unutilized;
Utilize the QRF in the response of disaster response-related activities to maximize the fund; and
Henceforth, strictly observe the guidelines regarding LDRRM PPAs under NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1, Section 1, Rule 18 of the IRR of RA No. 10121 and Section VI.6 of COA Memorandum No. 2014-009.

We recommended that Management:
Ensure that the projects were extensively coordinated with the target beneficiaries through public consultations and that sites were inspected before proceeding to bid the City’s PPAs and eliminate the potential causes of suspension/cancellation of priority projects;
Conduct EPA to ensure optimal utilization of the fund and avoid projects that are bidded/awarded at the end of the year to maximize the delivery of service to constituents; and
Henceforth, strictly observe the guidelines on the appropriation and utilization of the 20% DF under DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017.

We recommended that Management require the CAD to:
Further analyze and record the withholding of the amounts deducted from the contractors, suppliers and employees, totaling P41,914,495.34 as a credit to the Due to BIR account to correct the negative balance of the SLs;
Constantly coordinate within their offices’ sections and with the HRMD to promptly establish the actual amount withheld from employees, contractors/suppliers and other entities for immediate remittance to the BIR; and
Henceforth, strictly comply with Section 2.58 of BIR Revenue Regulations No. 2-98 and BIR Revenue Regulations No. 11-2018 to avoid interest and penalties.

We recommended that the Management:
Require the CAD to conduct further analysis to establish the final balances and subsequently record the withholding of the GSIS premiums and loans deducted from employees in prior years totaling P8,485,993.00 as a credit to Due to GSIS account to correct the negative balances in the books;
Instruct the CAD to encourage their different divisions handling the GSIS transactions to constantly coordinate within their ranks and with the HRMD to promptly establish the actual amount withheld from employees for immediate recording and remittance to the GSIS; and
Henceforth, strictly comply with Sections 6(b) of the RA No. 8291, Paragraph 27 of IPSAS 1 and accounts definitions under Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-009.

We recommended that Management require the CAD to:
Extensively analyze and record the withholding of the amounts deducted from the employees totaling P1,265,311.45 by crediting the Due to Pag-IBIG account to correct the negative balance in the Books;
Constantly coordinate within their offices’ sections and with the HRMD to promptly establish the actual amount of contributions withheld from employees for immediate remittance to the HDMF; and
Henceforth, strictly comply with Item E.4 of the HDMF Circular No. 275, Implementing Guidelines on Employer Registration, Contribution, and Remittance under RA No. 9679, to avoid exposing the City employees to unnecessary penalties the HDMF may impose.

We recommended that :
The CAD–FAAD and CAD–PBRD to reconcile their records and prepare necessary adjusting entries to come up with the correct balance of the Due to PhilHealth and Due to PhilHealth Contribution accounts;
The CAD–FMCD and HRMD to closely coordinate for the submission of the lacking documents to fast track the review and processing of transactions; and
Henceforth, strictly observe Paragraph 27 of the IPSAS 1 and 
Section 10 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II.

We recommended that Management:
Immediately submit the lacking documents for verification and audit;
Coordinate with and require the PRC to submit liquidation reports detailing all amounts deducted from the total advance cash deposits of P32,272,000.00 as cited in letter D.5 of the MOA; and
Moving forward, strictly comply with Section 2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012, in the granting, utilization and liquidation of funds released to NGOs/POs.

We reiterated our recommendation that the:
City Accountant immediately enforce the settlement of all audit suspensions, disallowances and charges in compliance with Sections 5.4 and 7.1.1, Chapter II of COA Circular No. 20`09-006 dated September 15, 2009; and
Management to be more vigilant in the implementation of the existing accounting and auditing rules and regulations in the processing of claims to avoid possible audit suspensions and disallowances.

We recommended that the Management advise the Municipal Accountant report immediately the unreconciled variance amounting to ₱751,361.69 between the reported Cash in Bank, LCCA (SEF) amounting to ₱2,882,545.91 in the SEF Books and the adjusted Cash in Bank, LCCA (SEF) amounting to ₱2,131,184.22 per Bank Reconciliation Statement and submit to the Auditor the adjusting entry made, if any, to adjust the amount reported as at year-end.

We recommended that Management instruct:
The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer reconcile the difference of the eleven (11) outstanding checks between the amount recorded in the books and the amount as per Report of Checks Issued;
The Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entries after reconciling the amount and submit a copy of the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to the Office of the Auditor; and
The Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant immediately verify the Special Savings Account and recognize the same on the books of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Management of LGU Quezon advise:
The Municipal Treasurer in coordination with the Municipal Assessor prepare and furnish the Municipal Accountant a duly certified list of taxpayers showing the names and the amounts due and collectible based on updated Real Property Tax Account Register (RPTAR)/ Taxpayer’s Index Card to provide an accurate and reliable basis in setting up the Receivables from RPT and SET;
The Municipal Accountant draw a Journal Entry Voucher to record the RPT/SET Receivables established at the beginning of the year based on the submitted duly certified list of taxpayers with the amount due and collectible for the year and maintain the corresponding subsidiary ledgers (SL) to monitor tax collections and delinquencies; and to use the correct account title in setting up the RPT/SET Receivables for CY 2022; and
The Municipal Assessor undertake thorough review on the assessments of real properties within the Municipality for taxation so that the Accounting Department could take up a more realistic RPT/SET Receivables in the books of accounts.

We recommended that the Management:
advise the Municipal Accountant stop the practice of recording immediately the procured supplies and materials to expense account; instead he should record all purchases  of supplies and materials regardless of whether or not they are consumed within the accounting period as an Inventory, except those purchased through petty cash fund, following the perpetual inventory method and an inventory control account should be maintained for each inventory item pursuant to Section 114 of the Manual on NGAS, Volume I; likewise, he shall maintain Supplies Ledger Cards per stock number; Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards for each category of assets; and Real Property Ledger Cards for land;
advise the Municipal General Services Officer (MGSO) number each type of supplies and maintain Stock Cards per stock number; he shall consolidate weekly the RIS for which supplies and materials were issued using the SSMI. The SSMI together with the original copy of the RIS shall be submitted to the Municipal Accountant, who shall compute the cost of supplies issued and ending inventory using the moving average method as provided under Section 115 of the same Manual and prepare the JEV to record the expenditures using the appropriate expense accounts; and
require the MGSO submit the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) and Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) for the result of inventory of supplies or property and physical count of PPE, respectively, to the Auditor concerned not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year.

We recommended that the Management advise:
The Municipal Accountant (1) draw a Journal Entry Voucher on the transfer of the unexpended balance of QRF and DRRMF-MOOE; (2) to disclose in the CY 2021 and succeeding years Notes to Financial Statements the details on the receipt and utilization of LDRRMF of the Municipality using the format as required under Item 5.1.16 of COA Circular 2012-002; and (3) to maintain a separate RAAO for QRF, MOOE and CO of LDRRMF; and
The LDRRMO use the required format on the Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF as required under Item 5.1.5 of COA Circular 2012-002 and submit the report on or before the 15th day after the end of each month through the LDRRMC and the Local Development Council to the COA auditor of the LGU.

We recommended that the Management require the Municipal Accountant maintain a special account in the general fund for the operation of the Local Economic Enterprises which can be used to formulate Management decisions whether to continue the operations of their respective LEEs.

We recommended that the Management justify/explain the incurrence of cash overdraft on the Trust Fund of the Municipality. Likewise, we recommend the prompt remittance of the unutilized trust funds due to other government agencies to avoid misappropriation thereof.

We recommended that the Management:
Require collectors turn over/remit all collections daily and intact to the Municipal Treasurer in compliance with Section 69 of P.D. 1445 and Item A of Section 60 of LTOM;
Require the Municipal Treasurer to closely supervise the collecting officers and monitor daily collections and remittances; and
Strengthen internal control over collections and deposits. To avoid holding big amount of money in the hands of Collectors, consider the following:
Promulgate and implement a policy on the turn-over of daily collections by the collectors to the Municipal Treasurer. A cut-off may be established on the time of turn-over that will give the Municipal Treasurer ample time to deposit the turned-over collections;
Likewise, established a cut-off in depositing daily collections to the bank by the Municipal Treasurer. This is to avoid having large balances of daily collections in the hands of Municipal Treasurer; and
Promulgate and implement a policy that will penalize holding of collections for several days in the hands of either the collectors or the Municipal Treasurer.

We recommended that the Management justify/explain why the above-mentioned expenditures were charged to the financial assistance from the Office of the President. Likewise, we recommended that Management thoroughly review all Memorandum of Agreement wherein the Municipality is a signatory and ensure that both parties affixed their signature thereon, in order to bind each party in accordance with the provisions in the MOA.

We recommended that the Management adhere with the provisions of COA Circular 2004-006 and COA Circular 2021-001 on the use of RER, otherwise, payments that are supported with RER instead of OR will be suspended/disallowed in audit, if applicable. Likewise, refrain from entering into contract with suppliers that are unregistered to BIR in order to support the campaign of the National Government on tax collection.

We recommended that the Management submit justification/explanation on why there was no separate bank account for SFP and why the Management opted to disburse the amount received from DWSD by distributing cash to DCWs instead of conducting a competitive bidding on the procurement of goods. Further, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant should observe the proper recording of transactions pertaining to the liquidations of fund transfer from NGAs.

We recommended that the Management advise the Municipal Accountant ensure that a copy of an approved POW be attached to the disbursement voucher to support the processing of the release of the funds to the implementing agency to establish monitoring of the proper utilization of the funds. Further, we recommended the Management to refrain from transferring of funds to Barangays if the latter do not have the capacity to hold competitive bidding as required under Section 10 of the Revised IRR of R.A No. 9184. Moreover, advise the Municipal Engineer and the MPDC to coordinate and to establish proper monitoring on the different projects/programs/activities implemented or monitored by the Municipality.

We recommended that the Management refrain from charging the wages of job order workers to the 20% DF. However, for CY 2022, we recommended to charge the wages of job orders of greening program to the Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses (MOOE) under the General Fund of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Management:
Refrain from appropriating funds to unrelated projects and activities of disaster risk reduction and management and adhere to the provisions of the NDRRMC, DBM and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013;
Revise the LDRRMP and AIP for CY 2022 to reprogram the above mentioned PPAs to disaster preparedness related projects, programs and activities and submit to the Auditor’s Office the copy of the revised LDRRMP and AIP for CY 2022; and
Explain/ justify why the above mentioned PPAs were funded under the LDRRMF to forestall the issuance of Notice of Disallowance on the amount disbursed for CY 2021.

We recommended that the Management require the Accounting Department to ensure that taxes are withheld and deducted from payment of honoraria of BAC members, TWG and BAC Secretariat in accordance with the Revised Withholding Tax Table and the same amount are remitted to the BIR within the prescribed period.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Management through the Municipal Accountant submit the necessary reports on timely basis and strictly comply with the provisions in COA Circular No 2009-006 and PD 1445 in order to avoid imposition of the sanctions provided therein.

We recommended that the Management advise the Municipal Accountant strictly deduct the loan amortization due from each employee based on GSIS billings. Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant explain/justify this basis in recording the account Due to GSIS and why the amount of ₱340,982.45 was not reflected in the Due to GSIS account as at December 31, 2021 despite the fact that billing statement from GSIS were received monthly by the Office of the Municipal Accountant.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Management prioritize the production of the Electrical Plan required by ISELCO for the immediate installation of the additional transformer necessary for the operation of said X-ray machine. We further recommended that the Municipal Planning and Development Officer adopt proper planning including all the necessary measures before any procurement to ensure that anything procured would be immediately utilized.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management establish specific guidelines in the granting of financial assistance for uniformity and fairness. Likewise, the members of the Sangguniang Bayan should stop the practice of giving financial assistance to its constituents regardless of source of fund as it is not among their mandated powers, duties and functions under Section 447 of RA 7160 as well as to avoid possible misuse of government funds.
We recommended to Management that the Petty Cash Fund be maintained under the imprest system in compliance with Section 48 of NGAS, Volume I.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer provide the necessary information in the Cash Disbursement Books and Report of Disbursements as prescribed by the Manual on the New Government Accounting System, Volume II.

We recommended that Management continue the practice of depositing collections regularly to comply with the rules and regulations as mandated by Section 69 of P.D. No. 1445 and Section 32 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I on collections and deposits.
We recommended that the Accountable Officer accomplish properly and completely the Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms to ensure accuracy thereof and to promote accountability.

We reiterated our previous audit recommendation that the Municipal Accountant thoroughly review the transactions which resulted in the negative balances of accounts and accordingly prepare adjustments to establish the accuracy and valuation thereof.

We recommended to Management the following:
that after the complete physical inventory of municipal properties and the preparation of RPCPPE, the same must be reconciled with the subsidiary ledger cards and property cards. Thereafter, the reconciled report must be submitted to the audit team for evaluation; and
the Municipal Accountant and Municipal General Services Officer to prepare and maintain subsidiary ledger cards and property cards, respectively.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
update and provide depreciation to the affected PPE accounts in order to present their reasonable book values in the financial statements in conformity with the provisions of IPSAS 17;
provide Subsidiary Ledgers of PPEs which includes relevant information such as date of purchase or availability for use, salvage values, useful life, etc. to facilitate the computation and verification of depreciation expenses; and
abide with COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 and follow the procedures in the derecognition of non-existing PPEs without available record of accountability.

We reiterated to Management our previous audit recommendation that:
the Municipal Engineer provide the Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer with the complete description and segregation of road components for those with data on construction cost. For those not posted in the registry but without identified cost and those included in the RPCRNS but not in the registry, the fair value must be used in determining the cost of road components and thereafter shall provide the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects;
the Municipal Accountant depreciate the carrying amount of existing roads and its components to reflect the asset in its used condition and accordingly adjust the amount recorded in the Road Network account supported by the RPCRNS, Registry Infrastructure for Roads and working paper on the determination of the depreciated replacement cost per registry. Subsequently, at the end of each year, a lapsing schedule for the computation of depreciation for each depreciable road component must be prepared as basis for recording depreciation expenses and the required Local Road Network Ledger Card for each road network shall likewise be maintained; and
the MGSO at the end or every accounting period to render a Report on Local Road Network using the pro-forma schedule in Annex A of same Circular and maintain local road inventory, Road Map and complete Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components.

We recommended that Management:
expedite the implementation of all on – going programmed and funded projects under the 20% DF to optimize utilization thereof and to ensure timely delivery of intended benefits for the constituents of the municipality;
continuously monitor, coordinate, and evaluate PPAs to ensure the completion of most urgent, needed, appropriate and eligible development projects; and
continuously observe the policies and guidelines in the appropriation and utilization of the 20% DF as enjoined by DBM-DOF-DILG-JMC No. 1, S. 2020

We recommended that Management:
ensure the allocation and utilization of LDRRMF pursuant to the policies and guidelines primarily embodied in NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1; and
thru the LDRRMC, continuously monitor, coordinate and evaluate PPAs to ensure the completion of most urgent, needed, appropriate and eligible projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
prepare the necessary adjusting entry to the affected accounts to ensure fair presentation of financial statements;
transfer unexpended balance of DRRMF other than those appropriated for capital outlays to the Trust Fund and recognized as Trust Liabilities – DRRMF; and
abide with the guidelines set forth under Items 5.1.10 and 5.1.11 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
prepare the necessary adjusting entry to the affected accounts to ensure fair presentation of financial statements; and
abide with the guidelines set forth under Item 5.1.11 and Annex C(A)(6) of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

We recommended that Management:
require the consultants / to pay to the Bureau of Internal Revenue (BIR) the amount of deficiency withholding tax and then submit proofs of payment to the auditor; otherwise, it shall be disallowed in audit; and
to comply with the withholding tax rate of ten percent (10%) for management and technical consultants; otherwise, penalties and interest shall be personally liable to those responsible for withholding and remittance as stated in Section 247(b) of the 1997 NIRC.

We reiterated our previous audit recommendation that:
the Accounting Office comply with the provision Section 114 of P.D. No 1445 and Section 4 (e) of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume I, so that all transactions would be properly documented and recorded. With this, all transactions can be readily verified and reconciled with the amount appearing in the financial statements. Thus, the accuracy and reliability of the financial statements will be achieved; and
the Management consider hiring more personnel to augment the staff requirements of the Accounting Office.

We reiterated our previous audit recommendation that:
the Municipal Accountant conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of the receivables and determine the concerned debtors to ascertain their collectability. If proven to be no longer collectible, request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts and attach required documents prescribed under COA Circular No. 2016-005; and
in the future, for transactions involving receivables, the Municipal Accountant should maintain subsidiary ledgers in accordance with Section 4 (e) of the NGAS to ensure complete information on the accounts and to facilitate the collection thereof.

We reiterated our previous audit recommendation that the Municipal Accountant:
analyze the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts, trace back differences and the nature of the accounts;
prepare the necessary adjusting entries to bring the correct balances of the accounts; and
maintain subsidiary ledger as necessary and include disclosure in the Notes to FS for fair presentation of Financial Statements

We recommended that the concerned Municipal Officials/SB members return the excessive and irregularly disbursed funds, otherwise, a Notice of Disallowance shall be issued. Further, management should observe the guidelines set by DBM LBC  No.  103  in  computing  the amount of RATA to be paid to  prevent  irregular  disbursements  of government funds.
We strongly recommended that the LGU stop the practice of paying RATA in advance to conform to the provisions of Section 88 of P.D. 1445 and DBM LBC No. 103.

We recommended that Management through:
the Bids and Awards Committee to attach all the documentary requirements as provided by COA Circulars 2012-001 and 2009-001 in the Disbursement Vouchers;
the BAC secretariat to submit copies of the bidding documents of all perfected contracts for FY 2021; and
the BAC and the Municipal Accountant, ensure that all perfected contracts and their respective supporting documents including those required in auditorial/technical review be submitted to the Audit Team within five (5) days from the execution thereof to facilitate immediate review and evaluation as required by COA Circulars No. 96-010 and 2009-001.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the designated officer responsible to (i) prepare and submit the Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances together with required attachments for all the donations in-kind received in CY 2021; and (ii) comply with the reportorial requirement to NDRRMC through the OCD for all donations in-kind received during quarantine period.

We reiterated that the Local Chief Executive to require the (a) Acting Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to reconcile their records at least quarterly to immediately detect any error/discrepancy and settle the variances noted as of year-end; and revisit the past records (i) to substantiate the propriety of adjustment for reconciling items; and (ii) settle the dormancy of the three accounts or institute their closure, if warranted.

We reiterated that the Local Chief Executive require the (a) Inventory Committee to (i) conduct physical inventory of all PPE of the Municipality on or before January 31 of each year as and immediately submit the related reports to the Office of the Auditor; and (ii) reconcile the resultant inventory reports with the Accounting records; and (b) designated Property Office and Municipal Accountant to (i) maintain complete PPE records such as PPE ledger cards and property cards for the proper control and monitoring of the agency properties.; and (ii) exert more efforts in locating documents of the unidentifiable PPEs.  We further recommended that the LCE direct the (a) Municipal Accountant and Property Officer to consider the guidelines on one-time cleansing of PPE accounts to render a reliable PPE information.

We reiterated that the Local Chief Executive should instruct the Municipal Accountant to revisit the prior year transactions and conduct the reconciliation of the balances of Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts and effect the necessary adjustments as warranted to bring their balances into agreement.

We reiterated our prior years recommendation and  the Management agreed to continue its effort to observe prudence in cash management by introducing austerity measures on its daily operations and providing sufficient cash back-up for due and demandable obligations.

We recommended that Local Chief Executive (a) refrain from including ineligible expenditure items to be charged against the Development Fund (DF); (b) instruct the Municipal Engineer and Municipal Planning Development Officer to monitor and supervise the implementation of the different development projects of the municipality to ensure its timely completion; and (c) instruct the Local Development Council (LDC) through the Municipal Planning and Development to consider the immediate reprogramming to other priority undertakings of projects that are determined to be unviable for implementation during the budget year.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to (i) transfer all the unexpended/unobligated balances of Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund to a special trust fund as required in NDRRMC, DBM and DILG JMC No. 2013-1 for monitoring of the utilization and assurance of the availability of said fund to support disaster related activities of the Municipality, and (ii) conduct reconciliation of the records pertaining to unexpended DRMMF in the previous years.

Further, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) instruct the LDRRM Officer to comply with the planning and reporting for LDRRM activities as provided for under NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013 and COA Circular No. 2012-002; (b) require the HRM Officer  to comply with the filling up of the vacant position in the LDRRMO in accordance with NDRRMC, DILG, DBM and CSC JMC No. 2014- 1 to effectively carry out the DRRM activities and programs; (c) instruct the HRM Officer and LDRRM Officer to revisit Section 5.1 of NDRRMC, DILG, DBM and CSC JMC No. 2014-1 and designate specific responsibility to each position such as (1) administration and training; (2) research and planning; and (3) operations and warning to ensure that all necessary tasks are accomplished to further assure attainment of the mandate of DRRM Office.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the (a) Bids and Awards Committee; BAC Secretariat, and Technical Working Group to (i) work on the completion of the documentary deficiencies and submit the same to the Audit Team; (ii) enhance the transparency on procurements by complying the publicity and notification requirements ;and (b) Municipal Accountant to strengthen her review function on the requisite documentation of disbursements for procurements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to submit immediately the unsubmitted disbursement voucher including its supporting documents.  Henceforth, strictly comply with the submission within the 10th day of the following month as stated in COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive and the Local School Board (LSB) agreed to (a) properly plan its annual programs/projects/activities and maximize the utilization of SEF to attain its objectives; (b) instruct the Inventory Committee to exercise the physical inventory of all properties booked under SEF and prepare a Report thereon; (c) require the Accounting Office and the Property Officer to properly maintain subsidiary ledger cards and property cards for all assets procured out of SEF; and (d) coordinate with the appropriate DepEd officials on fixing the accountability of the recipients/custodian of SEF property.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the (a) GAD Focal point System, through the GAD Focal Person, to continue its efforts to improve gender mainstreaming and  adherence/compliance with the provisions of relevant laws and regulations; (b) instruct the GAD Focal Point Person to use the Harmonized Gender and Development Guidelines (HGDG) in the attribution of costs of regular program/project to GAD and ensure that the prescribed budget level is satisfied; and (c) henceforth, aim to attain the full implementation and impose close monitoring of GAD activities indicated in the GPB to fully address the identified gender issues within the locality.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the concerned employees/disbursing officers to observe the pertinent provisions of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, particularly on the proper preparation of liquidation reports/report of disbursement for cash advances.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive continue to (a) exert best efforts in implementing its COVID-19 Vaccination Program in view of the Philippine National Deployment and Vaccination Plan for COVID-19 Vaccines guidelines; (b) intensify the information campaign to the public to spread awareness of COVID-19 vaccine benefits to achieve the Herd Immunity as a way to fight the said virus.

We recommended and the Management agreed to continue to comply with the BIR rules and regulations on the withholding and remittance of withheld taxes to help the BIR in raising its revenue. Furthermore, the Accounting Unit should investigate the occurrence and settle the negative balance in Due to BIR account under Trust Fund and make the proper book adjustment, if warranted.

We recommended and the Management agreed to continue complying with the GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG regulations on the withholding and remittance of employees’ and employer contributions and loan amortizations of employees to the respective agencies to assure all its members of the social security benefits due them.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the General Services Officer to determine the value of all the PPE items in the RPCPPE and present the said report per type of PPE as required in the MNGAS.
observations.managementAction: The management submitted RPCPPE to the Audit Team as of December 31, 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Agriculturist to submit physical count/inventory of the breeding stocks and prepare and render the report thereon complete with supporting documents as proof of the status/condition of the animals.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Agriculturist submitted the physical count/inventory of the breeding stocks.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. OIC-Municipal Assessor to accelerate and exert more effort on the ongoing general revision of real properties in order to provide the MT the complete and updated Assessment Roll; and
b. MT to submit to the Municipal Accountant the Certified List of Taxpayers showing their names and amounts due and collectible for the year so that the RPT/SET Receivables can be adjusted to reflect the correct balances.
observations.managementAction:
The OIC-Municipal Assessor provided the MT a complete and updated Assessment Roll.
The MT submitted the Certified List of Taxpayers to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. revert those account whose owners/creditors replied but was not able to submit, upon request, the required legal documents; and secure from the SB a resolution with a condition that appropriation shall be made in the event that they were able to provide those documents; and
b. continue to exert more effort to locate the owners/creditors and confirm the account and request them to submit documents pertaining to their claims with the Municipality. If creditors will not be able to support their claims, revert the account to the Unappropriated Surplus of the GF, subject to an authority from the SB through a Resolution with a condition that appropriation shall be made in the event that a claim arises in the future pursuant to the provisions of Section 98 of PD No. 1445.
observations.managementAction:
Adjusting entries were made under JEV no. 100-2021-12-00086 dated December 31, 2020.
Locating of the owners/creditors and confirm the account and request them to submit documents pertaining to their claims with the Municipality. If creditors will not be able to support their claims, revert the account to the Unappropriated Surplus of the GF, subject to an authority from the SB through a Resolution with a condition that appropriation shall be made in the event that a claim arises in the future pursuant to the provisions of Section 98 of PD No. 1445.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to expedite and continue efforts in providing the details/breakdown of the said accounts and remit the same to the aforementioned member beneficiaries so that they may avail of the privileges and benefits due them.
observations.managementAction: The reconciliation of the Payroll and remittances were already made.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the General Services Officer-Designate to continue his effort in completing and submitting legal documents required by the GSIS pertaining to the assessment of the insurable properties and the provision of its insurance.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality, being fifth class Municipality is exempted under RA No. 656.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the OIC-Municipal Assessor to:
a. accelerate the titling of the two remaining lots whose titling process were already commenced and submitted to the concerned agencies and waiting for the release of Certificate of Title; and
b. immediately start the process of the titling for the six lots where legal documents were found, continue exerting more efforts in locating the documents of the five lots and make all the necessary actions/representations with the DENR and RD in the titling of the lots.
observations.managementAction:
The titling process of the remaining lot is still on-going as of year-end.
Locating of legal documents pertaining to the five lots are still on-going as of year-end.
observations.reason:
The titling process of the remaining lot is still on-going as of year-end.
Locating of legal documents pertaining to the five lots are still on-going as of year-end.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer to accelerate the modification of the construction layout/plan so that the said project will be implemented for the benefit of the constituents.
observations.managementAction:
The construction plan/layout based on their information was already prepared.
Follow-up coordination from the Department of Environment and Natural Resources to the Municipality of Quezon for the finalization of the plan/layout.
observations.reason: Coordination from the Department of Environment and Natural Resources to the Municipality of Quezon for the finalization of the plan/layout.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Health Officer (MHO) to adhere with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-001 to ensure the safety and quality of the procured drugs and medicines.
observations.managementAction: The supplier submitted the certification of drug distributorship from the authorized distributor/manufacturer.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to release the RPT share of the component Barangays promptly within five days after the end of each quarter.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality distributed the RPT share of the Barangays promptly within five days after the end of the period.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a) instruct the Municipal Accountant to immediately transfer the unexpended balances of the Due to NGAs held in Trust to the General Fund; and b) require the Sangguniang Bayan to enact an Appropriation Ordinance in line with the policy/guidelines set-forth under DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 2.
observations.managementAction:
Liquidation to Department of Health was made under Journal Entry Voucher No. 300-2021-03-01021 dated March 4, 2021 amounting to P5,062.21.
SB Resolution is no longer needed due to low COVID-19 cases and IATF health protocols.
observations.reason: Completion of the project which needs additional materials and budget are necessary in order to comply with the MOA between the DA and the Municipality.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. maximum utilization of the GAD funds through the implementation of GAD-related programs and projects in order to attain the objective for which funds were provided; and
b. OIC–MPDC to expedite the establishment of CBMS and institutionalize a GAD database with adequate and systematically gathered sex-disaggregated data.
observations.managementAction:
GAD Budget was optimally utilized as of year-end.
Partially established CMBS and disaggregated data. Completion of CBMS and GAD data base is still on-going as of year-end.
observations.reason: Completion of CBMS and GAD data base is still on-going as of year-end.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality, through the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board, follow-up the status of the review of the revised plan of the proposed Sanitary Landfill and secure the approval of its 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan from the National Solid Waste Management Commission pursuant to the provisions under the above cited regulations.
observations.managementAction: Established partially a new 10-year SWMP for 2023-2032. Issuance of ECC is still on-going due to uploading of Portal. The closure of controlled open-dumpsite and converting the same to Materials Recovery Facility (MRF) and Sanitary Landfill has been made. We also recommended the Municipality, through the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board, follow-up the status of the review of the revised plan of the proposed Sanitary Landfill and secure the approval of its 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan from the National Solid Waste Management Commission pursuant to the provisions under the above cited regulations.
observations.reason: On-going completion of a new 10-year SWMP for 2023-2032 until it is approved by the National Solid Waste Management Commission.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. HRMO-Designate to exert more efforts and prioritize the updating of the salary adjustments and service records of the employees in the GSIS database; and
b. Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to prioritize the remittance of the current and partial arrears GSIS premiums in the amount of P1,985,119.65 to the GSIS upon reconciliation/updating of the employee records.
observations.managementAction:
The Employees service records in the GSIS database were already updated.
Remittance of the current and partial arrears GSIS premiums of the employees were already made.
Remittance of the remaining premiums in arrears of the employees are still on-going as of year-end.
observations.reason: Remittance of the remaining premiums in arrears of the employees are still on-going as of year-end.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Provincial Governor require the OIC-Provincial Accountant to:
a. revisit the reconciling items without available documents to determine the appropriate action/disposition to pave the way for their elimination;
b. verify and analyze the long-reported reconciling items of P152,852.38 to establish their validity and correctness; and
c. prepare the adjusting entry reverting the stale checks totaling P1,914,090.49 and recognizing the corresponding payable account.
observations.managementAction:
The OIC-Provincial Accountant made an adjustment on reconciling items of current year and prior years. Reconciliation Statement prepared was reconciled.
The Provincial Accounting Office has already verified the long-reported reconciling items and was already adjusted.
The OIC-Provincial Accountant adjusted stale checks amounting to P1,914,090.49 and recognized the appropriate payable account.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor require the OIC-Provincial Accountant to enforce liquidation of all cash advances at year end in accordance with Item 5.8 of the Commission on Audit (COA) Circular No. 97-002. Otherwise, the necessary sanction to enforce its settlement such as the withholding of the salary to be applied to the unliquidated cash advances shall be enforced.
observations.managementAction:
The Provincial Accountant made adjustment on prior year and current year. Some accountable officers liquidated the cash advance granted to them.
The OIC-Provincial Accountant wrote off unliquidated cash advance amounting to P44,064 and other receivables amounting to P462,379.56 per COA Decision No. 2021-01 dated December 9, 2021.
Unliquidated cash advance amounting to P519,942.00 has pending complaint/case, hence was not requested for write-off. The balance of other receivables amounting to P499,651.48 pertains to cash advance which was reclassified to other receivables and a subject of case with the Ombudsman.
observations.reason: The amount of P71,208.00 is for liquidation pending submission of supporting documents while the amount of P519,942.00 will be requested for write-off since the accountable officer could not be reached in the address on file.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor require the OIC-PGSO to submit the Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) and the List of Recipients to the Provincial Accounting Office. Also, instruct the OIC-Provincial Accountant to adjust the said account accordingly.
observations.managementAction: The OIC-Provincial Accountant made adjustment upon receipt of the list of recipients. The OIC-PGSO continuously sends reminders to the accountable officials for the list of recipients and will conduct quarterly reconciliation of the inventories for distribution and for consumption with accounting records starting CY 2022. A form was improvised to keep track of current year’s balance while working on the prior year’s liquidation and will require the Accounting Office to mark the documents received for proper monitoring.
observations.reason: Reconciliation is on-going for the remaining balance.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Provincial Governor:
a) Direct the Provincial Engineer to facilitate and hasten the titling of these parcels of land and submission to Accounting and register the same with the Registry of Deeds to ensure absolute ownership thereof; and
b) Require the OIC-Provincial Accountant to effect the reclassification to the appropriate Property, Plant and Equipment account relative to the transfer of completed projects to the Buildings and Other Structures account with the corresponding adjustment in the books of account and recognition of accumulated depreciation.
observations.managementAction:
The OIC-Provincial Accountant adjusted CIP relative to repairs and maintenance to Prior Period Adjustment. CIP pertaining to the purchase of luminous tiles and ceramics in CY 2006 that was set-up as payables was already paid last January 9, 2007, hence adjustment will be made. Adjustment for the completed project was not made immediately, as the final payment upon verification was paid under the Trust Fund book. Reclassification of accounts will be made.
A letter was sent to the Provincial Engineering Office (PEO) to verify the status of those infrastructure projects. Two projects have not yet been paid but were already completed and the contractors are in the process of claiming the final payment. Adjustments including the recording of related depreciation will be made once necessary supporting documents have been received. Communication with PEO will be periodically undertaken for the timely updating and proper monitoring of projects included in the CIP accounts.
observations.reason: For the remaining projects, communication with PEO is on-going; contractors are in the process of claiming final payments for some completed projects.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct the OIC-Provincial Accountant to review the Due to GSIS account, and make the necessary adjustment pertaining to the negative balances, to eliminate erroneous recordings in the said account.
observations.managementAction:
The OIC-Provincial Accountant conducted periodic analysis of subsidiary ledgers and it was found out that some employees have duplicate ledgers. Reports Division and Payroll and Remittance Division are coordinating as to the official ledgers so that the excel files being used in posting to eNGAS will be revised to prevent increase in the negative balances with the same cause. Correcting entries have been made but errors recurred. Other causes are still being evaluated with the intention that a corresponding system of posting may be devised.
P685,732.48 out of P2,589,254.84 negative balance have been eliminated and corrected. Correcting entries have been made, but errors recur due to the same reasons mentioned. Other causes are still being evaluated.
observations.reason: Continuous and periodic reconciliation is being undertaken; errors recur due to duplicate ledgers and posting processes; further evaluation ongoing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor require the OIC-Provincial Accountant to avail of the remedy to clean its books by requesting for an authority to write-off the unliquidated cash advance and other receivables, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005.
observations.managementAction:
The Provincial Accountant’s Office wrote off unliquidated cash advance balances. The Provincial Government’s Office is continuously exerting effort to request for write-off of unliquidated cash advance and other receivables amounting to P44,064 and P462,379.56, respectively, per COA Decision No. 2021-01 dated December 9, 2021.
Unliquidated cash advance amounting to P519,942.00 has pending complaint/case, hence was not requested for write-off. The balance of other receivables amounting to P499,651.48 pertains to cash advance which was reclassified to other receivables and a subject of case with the Ombudsman.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendations that the Provincial Governor to:
a) Direct the OIC-Provincial Treasurer to compel the accountable officers to promptly remit collections to prevent possible misuse, theft, and misappropriation of government funds and to facilitate timely recording;
b) Authorize the cashiers of the district hospitals to deposit directly collections to the nearest depository bank (authorized depository banks of the Province) to reduce risk of loss in transit as well as to ensure prompt deposit of collections; and
c) Require the Chief of Hospitals to ensure and monitor that collections of the hospitals are remitted by the accountable officer on time and intact.
observations.managementAction: The Office of the Provincial Treasurer is continuously exerting effort to remind and compel collecting officers to promptly remit the collections. It was agreed that the collections will be deposited to the nearest depository bank. In a meeting held last July 10, 2021, it was agreed that the chief of hospitals will address the issue of late deposits and will instruct the accountable officers to comply with the regulations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor require the OIC-Provincial General Services Officer (PGSO) to hasten the titling of these parcels of land and register the same with the Registry of Deeds to ensure absolute ownership thereof.
observations.managementAction:
Title serves as a valid proof of claim under Section 5(g.2) of PD No. 10752 and enough proof that the Provincial Government does not necessitate the processing of Transfer of Title, hence the land is only partially affected.
The encumbrance entered in all the Title serves as a valid proof of claim under Section 5(g.2) of PD No. 10752 and enough proof that the Provincial Government does not necessitate the processing of Transfer of Title, hence the land is only partially affected.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct the OIC-Provincial Accountant to:
