a) Verify accounts with identified creditors through confirmation, whether indeed they are the valid claimants/creditors and are supported with documents for said claims; and
b) Certify the outstanding accounts payable, for which there are no valid claimants/creditors and no supporting documents and revert the verified amounts to the Unappropriated Surplus by preparing the necessary Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) as basis for recording in the books of accounts of the Province.
observations.managementAction:
Accounts Payable amounting to P1,307,606.37 was reverted to the Unappropriated Surplus on May 3, 2021. The Provincial Accountant verified accounts with identified creditors and adjusted accordingly. For the remaining balance of P725,216.29, the Accounting Office is in the process of verifying accounts with identified creditors.
Certification was made as to the accounts payable which was reverted to the Unappropriated Surplus.
observations.reason: For the remaining balance of P725,216.29, the Accounting Office is in the process of verifying accounts with identified creditors. Analysis will be conducted to eliminate remaining dormant accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct all the concerned persons/departments to submit their action plans and status of implementation to his Office and consolidate the same using the AAPSI form, either in printed form or by way of electronic document for submission to the Audit Team within 60 days from receipt of the AAR.
observations.managementAction: The concerned offices furnished the Provincial Governor of the action taken and submitted the AAPSI within the prescribed period. The OIC-Provincial Administrator consolidated and submitted the AAPSI within the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Provincial Governor require the Provincial Engineer to:
a) Submit at the beginning of the year the list of all on-going projects/programs/activities (PPAs) and those that are to be implemented during the year; b) Inform the Audit Team within ten days after the award of PPAs or before its start that the appropriate project signboards and/or public notices were already posted for validation; and c) regularly submit the quarterly monitoring report with complete details.
observations.managementAction: The Provincial Engineer submitted the list of on-going projects at the beginning of the year. The Audit Team was informed of the award of PPAs and its project signboards. Quarterly monitoring reports with complete details were submitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor require the OIC-PGSO to submit to the Audit Team the Monthly Report of Official Travels and Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption for each vehicle, in compliance with COA Circular No. 77-61, for review and evaluation.
observations.managementAction: The PGSO regularly submit the Monthly Report of Official Travels and Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption for vehicles. Monthly Reports were prepared and submitted to the Audit Team for review and evaluation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor instruct the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) to prepare the GAD ARs and ensure that this is completed and submitted to the Audit Team and DILG for verification, review and assessment by the offices concerned to warrant that the GAD activities and accomplishments are in accordance with the approved plan and budget.
observations.managementAction: The GFPS prepared and submitted the GAD ARs to the Audit Team as stated in Section C.8 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 for verification, review and assessment by the offices concerned. The GAD ARs were submitted and assessed.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to: exhaust all possible means to obtain the supporting documents of the unrecorded disbursements and bank credit memoranda, and immediately transmit the same to the Municipal Accountant for recording thereof in the book of accounts; and prepare the Report of Checks Issued (RCI) in accordance with the guidelines provided under Section 83.3 of the LTOM.
We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer send a written notice to the payees concerned at least one month before a check becomes stale and the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjustments in the books for the long outstanding/stale checks in accordance with Section 189 of GAAM, Volume I and Section 83(b)(4) of the LTOM.
observations.managementAction:
The Acting Municipal Treasurer, as per instruction of the Municipal Mayor, searched all supporting documents of unreported disbursements and deposits from available files upon her assumption in office in 2021. The Acting Municipal Treasurer prepared the RCI, supported with the required documents, pursuant to the said regulations.
The Acting Municipal Treasurer verified all outstanding checks and sent written notice to payees concerned.
observations.reason: Material amount of stale checks still remained in the BRS as of December 31, 2021.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: thru, the Municipal Accountant, regularly send written notice to concerned accountable officers (AOs) to demand settlement of their outstanding cash advances; and consider withholding the salaries of AOs still connected with the Municipality who refuse to settle their accounts without justifiable reason.
The Municipal Accountant exert efforts to locate documents and previous records to facilitate reconciliation and/or determination of the causes of the unreconciled and negative cash advance balances of various AOs and thereafter effect the necessary adjustment in the books of accounts, as appropriate.
In all instances, the Municipal Mayor and the Municipal Accountant must ensure that the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 are strictly observed in the granting and liquidation of cash advances and see that all cash advances are fully liquidated at the end of the year.
observations.managementAction:
Management served demand letters to AOs with outstanding cash advances.
AOs still in service were reminded to settle their unliquidated balances, otherwise, management will enforce the withholding of their salaries.
The Municipal Accountant searched documents and previous records regarding unreconciled and negative cash advance balances, to help effect needed adjustments in the books of accounts, if needed.
Management stopped granting additional cash advance to AOs who still have unliquidated balances.
observations.reason: Adjustment in the books is yet to be effected this year 2022.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor reconsitute the Inventory Committee compose of adequate number of members to be able to complete the physical inventory in three months or less, with at least one member each from the Municipal Accountant’s Office and the General Services Office, and temporarily relieve the members from their regular duties to devote their full time in the conduct of the physical inventory taking until the same is completed.
The Inventory Committee, in coordination with the Acting General Services Officer (GSO), should be required to prepare a Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) containing, at least, the specific assignments/duties of the members, the cut-off date, and a schedule specifying the dates and locations of the inventory taking activities from start up to the targeted completion of the physical inventory, and to be approved by the Municipal Mayor, for submission to the Audit Team at least ten calendar days before the scheduled start of inventory taking activities.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the offices concerned to furnish the Acting GSO the detailed information pertaining to PPE items under their custody so that complete details may be included in the inventory of the PPE.
Further, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor designate a regular employee who will monitor compliance by the Inventory Committee, the Acting GSO, and the Municipal Accountant in compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2020-006 on the procedural guidelines for the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances.
After the cut-off date set by the Inventory Committee in the PIP, the Municipal Accountant and the Acting GSO should ensure that the proper accounting and reporting procedures for all acquisition/receipt/issue/transfer/ disposal of PPE pursuant to the relevant laws, rules and regulations on property and supply management are strictly followed.
Thereafter, the Municipal Mayor should ensure that physical inventory is undertaken annually and the reports thereon are submitted accordingly.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Mayor issued Executive Order (EO) creating the Inventory Committee.
The Acting GSO already prepared and submitted the PIP to the Audit Team.
The Municipal Mayor instructed and issued an office order for the purpose.
The Municipal Mayor already designated personnel who will monitor compliance of the Inventory Committee.
Management submitted the partial Report on the Physical Count of PPE (RPCPPE) for CY 2021 to the Audit Team.
observations.reason: The physical count was only partial, and reconciliation of the RPCPPE with relevant records was not yet started.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant support the general ledger (GL) controlling account with complete and updated subsidiary records to effectively control and monitor any movement in the Land account.
Moreover, the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Assessor should review the discrepancies in their records for its reconciliation.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor facilitate the immediate titling of real properties owned by the Municipality.
observations.managementAction:
The offices concerned already reviewed the discrepancies in their records for its reconciliation.
Same as above.
observations.reason: Funds for the immediate titling of real properties were not yet allocated by the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: create or reconstitute the Inventory Committee who will conduct an inventory of the local roads within the Municipality and submit the corresponding reports to the Audit Team for evaluation and verification; and enjoin the concerned Department Heads’ compliance with the requirements of COA Circular No. 2015-008 to facilitate the transfer of the local roads accounts from the RPI to the books of accounts and reflect the correct Road Networks account balance in the Statement of Financial Position.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Mayor already reconstituted the Inventory Committee.
The Inventory Committee submitted the Report of the Local Road Network (RLRN) and Road Map to the Audit Team on March 14, 2022.
observations.reason: The Report on the Physical Count of Local Road Network (RPCLRN) was not yet finalized for submission to the Municipal Accountant and the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) to: comply with the required posting of information and publicity of government PPAs in the municipal premises and at the projects’ sites; provide the Audit Team, at the beginning of the year, with the list of all government PPAs to be implemented for a particular year with the complete information such as: project name; implementing unit; office or division if it is not the agency as a whole; brief description of the PPAs; contractor or supplier, if any; mode of procurement; funding source; cost or approved budget; project duration including start and completion dates; and location; inform the Audit Team within ten days after the award of the projects or before the start of the PPAs that the appropriate project signboards and/or public notices are already posted at the agency premises and at the project sites; and submit monthly monitoring report on the implementation of all Municipality’s PPAs in accordance with COA Circular No. 2013-004 and ensure that the cost incurred to date indicated in the report reconcile with the records of the Municipal Accountant.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipality complied with the required posting of government PPAs.
Management submitted monitoring report either monthly or quarterly to the Audit Team.
The MPDC submitted regular status report of implementation of PPAs to the Municipal Mayor for his information.
Management submitted monitoring reports of implementation of PPAs to the Municipal Accountant for reconciliation.
observations.reason:
The required report was not yet submitted to the Audit Team.
Management still failed to inform the Audit Team on the posting of project signboards.
Delayed submission of monitoring reports and complete information were not provided therein.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendations that: The Local Development Council (LDC) and the MPDC together with the implementing offices concerned, in coordination with the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) (i) immediately look into the unimplemented projects under the 20% DF and determine the causes of their non-implementation; (ii) formulate / develop specific strategies/solutions to effectively implement development projects; (iii) consider formulating a timetable or a catch-up plan within which to complete the unimplemented projects; (iv) review the costing of projects programmed two to eight years ago to conform to the present conditions and costs; The Municipal Engineer (i) improve the planning and scheduling of work activities and manpower in order to facilitate completion of the Program of Work of unimplemented projects; (ii) ensure that plans are accurate and projects estimates are based on standard and realistic amounts; The MPDC (i) enhance monitoring of implementation of projects in coordination with the implementing offices; and (ii) submit regularly status reports of implementation to the Municipal Mayor for his information so that appropriate measures can be instituted to obtain optimal utilization of fund; The Municipal Mayor (i) ensure the full or optimum utilization of the 20% DF in accordance with Section 5 of DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1; (ii) ensure the effective and efficient implementation of the projects to maximize the developmental benefits of the intended beneficiaries; and (iii) convene MPDC, Municipal Engineer and other offices department heads concerned to strongly pursue the completion of the projects to be implemented the soonest time possible in order to achieve the objectives of the said projects in providing socio-economic benefits to the intended beneficiaries and remind them of their duties and responsibilities.
observations.managementAction:
Management conducted immediate meetings with the implementing agency concerned to install measures to address the delayed or non-implementation of PPAs.
Program of Works (POW) of some PPAs was revised to consider the costing of labor and materials and funds were realigned for the timely implementation of priority projects.
The Office of the Municipal Engineer reviewed and updated the plans and work schedules and constituted teams for project planning, implementation, repair and maintenance and monitoring and evaluation.
The MPDC issued office order assigning personnel who will conduct regular monitoring.
The Municipal Mayor met with the departments concerned to revisit the plans for projects funded out of the 20% DF and prioritize projects to be implemented.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (i) prioritize the filling in of the remaining three plantilla positions in the LDRRM Office in compliance with Section 12 of RA No. 10121 and NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC JMC No. 2014-1 dated April 4, 2014. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor (i) direct the MDRRMC, the MDRRMO and other personnel concerned to update the DRRM Plan with the inclusion of a Public Service Continuity Plan (PSCP); and (ii) explore feasible means, in view of funding constraints, to include in the LDRRM Fund Investment Plan the provision of additional trainings to LDRRM personnel, procurement of the aforementioned lacking equipment and tools, provision of adequate facilities for all evacuation centers, and construction of warehouse to comply with the minimum standards on disaster preparedness.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Mayor already directed the Human Resources Management Office (HRMO) to facilitate action of the Personnel Selection Board to fill up the vacant plantilla positions under the LDRRM Office.
The Municipality is still in the process of collating data from various departments and units for the formulation of PSCP.
The construction of facilities and the procurement of lacking equipment and tools were already considered and funds were provided in the Annual Budget.
observations.reason:
Delay in the filling up of vacant positions due to the adverse effect of the COVID-19 pandemic.
Most of the departments were not yet able to prepare their own PSCP for consolidation.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the: Members of the BAC and the requesting unit concerned to provide the kind of rice procured in the aforementioned POs for further validation of price reference for goods, and explanation/justification on the high price of medical supplies procured.
Moreover, the BAC members must ensure that the complete and detailed item description is included in the PO, in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 96-010. Henceforth, the members of the BAC are advised to be updated and adhere to the relevant laws, rules and regulations on procurement.
The Municipal Accountant, the members of the BAC, and other offices concerned to submit the lacking required documents to the Audit Team for further evaluation of the transactions pursuant to Section 4 (6) of PD No. 1445. The Municipal Accountant was also reminded to diligently review the DVs and supporting documents prior to approval and payment, as part of the Accounting Office’s internal audit function.
The Sanggunian Bayan to pass an ordinance creating SAGF for the BGCM for easy monitoring/tracking of the utilization of the allocation, pursuant to Item 3.7 of DBM LBC No. 125, and thereafter, the Municipal Accountant adjust the books by transferring all the BGCM transactions to the SAGF.
observations.managementAction:
The BAC members attended webinars to update them on procurement rules and regulations.
The Municipal Accountant already submitted the lacking supporting documents.
The Sanggunian Bayan already passed an ordinance creating SAGF for the BGCM and the Municipal Accountant already adjusted the books of accounts.
observations.reason:
The BAC failed to provide the kind of rice procured.
POs issued in CY 2021 were still lacking the complete specifications.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the: MPDC, with the assistance of the GFPS, to establish a complete and updated GAD database, and conduct gender analysis to serve as basis for addressing gender issues and concerns in GAD planning and budgeting for the ensuing years; and GFPS to ensure the timely submission of the GAD AR of the preceding year together with other documents required enumerated under Item C of the above-mentioned JMC.
observations.managementAction:
Municipal information system was created for the purpose funded under the GAD fund.
The Municipality already submitted to the Audit Team the GAD AR for CY 2021.
observations.reason: Community-based monitoring system (CBMS) is still ongoing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management: Draw journal entry vouchers to record all valid reconciling items that require adjustment and correction in the GL; and Henceforth, adhere to the regulations cited in COA Circular 96-011 relative to BRS of the City.
observations.managementAction: Draws Journal Entry Vouchers to record the reconciling items which have supporting documents. Management adhered to the regulations cited in COA Circular 96-011 relative to BRS of the City.
observations.reason: Reconciling items with no supporting documents are yet to be recorded by the CAD. Reiterated in Observation No. 01 page 98 of CY 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management: Send demand letter to HHPFI, PPHC and the landowner to pay immediately the amount of P33,145,000.00, P74,711,398.84, and P21,347,364.88, respectively; Reconcile/adjust the differences of P10,725,000.00, P19,724,325.28 and P224,325.28 or a net difference of P30,225,000.00 in the Subsidiary Ledger/General Ledger of Other Receivables-Bistekville 1 (HDMF-Habitat), Other Receivables-Bistekville 2 (HDMF- Phinma), and Other Receivables- Bistekville 2 (HDMF-Landowner), respectively; and Henceforth, conduct regular monitoring and analysis of receivable accounts to ensure collection when these become due and demandable as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2016-005.
observations.managementAction: Partially implemented; Drawn journal entries to record the adjustments on the accounts based on the data/documents already available. Fully implemented; Prepared JEV# 2020-12-029304 dated December 29, 2020, on the settlement of the accounts by the Socialized Housing beneficiaries. Management adhered to the recommendations.
observations.reason: Lack of material time to re-analyzat ion of the accounts because of the limited operational capacity and alternative work arrangement for the respective Departments of the City due to COVID-19 pandemic

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor: Enforce the liquidation of cash advances in accordance with the prescribed period pursuant to COA Circular 97-002; Instruct the City Accountant to decline the grant of additional CA to officers and employees unless their previous cash advances have been accounted and settled; Require all accountable officers to immediately liquidate their respective cash advances as soon as the purposes of which the advances were granted has been served and submit the required LRs and supporting documents to CAD; Require CAD to ensure that LRs and supporting documents are processed and recorded immediately; and Henceforth, comply strictly with COA Circular No. 97-002 in the granting, utilization, and liquidation of cash advances.
observations.managementAction: Partially implemented; The AOs and employees are always notified to liquidate their CAs within the prescribed period by sending them demand letters. Partially implemented; Drafted policies for the implementation of salary deduction for long outstanding unliquidated cash advances for active employees, composed of: 1. The Procedure for the Penalization of active Employees with Unliquidated Cash Advances 2. The Policy/Guidelines for Salary Deductions of City Officers & Employees who have Outstanding Cash Advances 3. Final Demand Letter for non-compliance 4. Final Demand Letter for partial compliance. Fully implemented; AOs and employees without the CAD-FAAD's Certification of No Unliquidated CA balance. All LRs and supporting documents are processed and recorded immediately upon receipts of such. The Management adheres to the recommendation to comply with COA Circular No. 97-002 in the granting, utilization, and liquidation, of cash advances.
observations.reason: There are still CAs that remained outstanding despite the Management actions.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management prepare necessary adjusting entry to reclassify the receipt of the Bayanihan Grant from Share from IRA to Transfers from National Government account.
observations.managementAction: Fully implemented; Management has already prepared JEV No. 2020-12-028045 dated December 29, 2020, to adjust/reclassify the receipt of the Bayanihan Grant Act from the Share from IRA. Have already requested the COA-Accounting Systems Development and Other Services Office to make the Transfers from NGAs account be available in the e-NGAS Facility, and once available, the necessary reclassification from the account-Subsidy from NGAs to the account-Transfers from NGAs shall be made.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management submit the lacking documents/justification enumerated to comply with the basic requirements for emergency procurements pursuant to GPBB Circular No. 01-2020 dated April 6, 2020, and with posting and reporting requirements stipulated in Item Nos. 4.1 and 4.4 of LBC No. 125 dated April 7, 2020.
observations.managementAction: Fully Implemented; Management had submitted the lacking documents as required in the observation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management: Strictly monitor the implementation of all planned LDRRM PPAs to ensure the achievement of the City’s goal and objectives in mitigating the impact of disaster; and Henceforth, strictly observe the guidelines regarding the implementation of LDRRM PPAs under COA Circular No. 2012-002, COA Memorandum No. 2014-009 and other related issuances
observations.managementAction: Fully implemented; Management considered the unimplemented projects in CY 2021. Fully implemented; Management adhered to the recommendations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City: Maximize the utilization/implementation of the GPB to attain its objective in accordance with Item 2.3 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC 2013-01; Monitor through the GFPS Executive Committee and TWG the effective and efficient implementation of the GAD PPAs and the judicious utilization of the GAD funds; Prepare GAD PPAs budget judiciously so as not to exceed its appropriation during projects implementation; Prepare the GPB and GAD AR in accordance with the format provided in Annexes D and E of PCW-DILG-DBM NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular Nos. 2013-01; Provide complete details in the GAD AR on the implementation of GAD related programs to determine the efficiency and effectiveness of intervention in addressing gender-related issues; Henceforth, strictly observe the guidelines regarding GAD PPAs under PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01, as amended by PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2016-01 and Section 305 (a) of RA 7160.
observations.managementAction: Partially Implemented; The management implemented the PPAs in the following year, subject to minimum health protocols due to COVID-19 Pandemic. Partially Implemented; The management implemented the PPAs in the following year, subject to minimum health protocols due to COVID-19 Pandemic. Fully Implemented; The advisory of the DILG dated April 3, 2020, on the “Easing the Review Process of the FY 2020 GPB of LGU’s in view of the Covid-19 states that the review of 2020 GPB by concerned high-level LGU and the DILG is temporarily suspended, provided, that the LGUs shall still submit a copy of the CY 2020 GPB to the concerned DILG Office highlighting the changes made. Fully Implemented; The Management adhered to the recommendation to include in the preparation of GAD AR complete details on the implementation of GAD related programs.
observations.reason: PPAs were not implemented because of the unsuccessful bidding due to the pandemic, and there was no ample time to proceed with an alternative mode of procurement. Further, the delay was due to the skeletal workforce of the personnel because of the lockdown and restrictions to avoid contagion of the virus.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management: Cause the immediate remittance of the remaining Green Fund maintained by retailers in the total amount of P259,919,389.96as of December 31, 2020, including interest, if any; and Strictly comply with the provisions under Quezon City Ordinance No. SP-2868, S-2019 dated September 30, 2019.
observations.managementAction: Partially Implemented; Collected P163.293M as of 12-23-2021. Fully Implemented; The CTO is exerting all efforts to require the remittance of all the remaining Green Funds.
observations.reason: Consideration of appeals of other retailers to remit on a staggered basis.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management: Remit the premium contributions and loan amortizations withheld from employees totaling P955,593.39; and Henceforth, strictly comply with Section 3, Rule VII of the IRR of R.A. No. 9679 dated July 21, 2009, on the remittances of premium contributions and loan payments.
observations.managementAction: Partially Implemented; Management coordinated with HRMD and concerned City Officials and employees regarding discrepancies in data between HRMD and PagIBIG. Fully Implemented; Also, Partial updating of data between HRMD and PagIBIG.
observations.reason: There are still premium contributions and loan amortizations withheld that are yet to be remitted. Also, partial updating of data is ongoing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management: Remit the tax of P50,175,158.76 withheld from officers/employees, contractors/ suppliers and other entities to the BIR; and Henceforth, ensure prompt and full remittance of taxes withheld pursuant to Section 2.58 of BIR Revenue Regulations No. 2-98 to avoid the incurrence of interest as defined under Section 11 of BIR Revenue Regulation No. 11-2018.
observations.managementAction: Partially Implemented; Started reconciliation of prior years' unremitted taxes. Fully implemented; Management has ensured prompt and full remittance of taxes withheld.
observations.reason: Reconciliat ion is still in process. Reiterated in Finding no. 15 page 144 in AAR CY 2021

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management: Remit immediately all unremitted premium contributions, consisting both the City’s and employees’ shares, to avoid unnecessary expense; Reclassify the amount of P23,277,877.98 from Due to Philhealth to Due to GOCCS account; and Henceforth, strictly observe Section 20(b), Rule III, Title III of the Revised IRR of the National Health and Section 5, Volume III of GAM for LGUs for the accounting of Due to Philhealth.
observations.managementAction: Partially Implemented; With partial compliance of unremitted premium contributions. Fully Implemented; The amount of P23,277,877.98 was reclassified from Due to Philhealth to Due to GOCCS account. Management adhered to the recommendations.
observations.reason: Reconciliat ion of unremitted premiums and payments is still in progress for the balance of P98,010.95.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management: Remit immediately the amount of P4,200,028.84 pertaining to unremitted premium contributions consisting both of City’s and employees’ shares and loan payments; and Henceforth, strictly comply with Sections 6(b) and 7 of the Republic Act No. 8291 dated May 30, 1997.
observations.managementAction: Partially Implemented; Remittances in the amount of P9,564,382.71 were made from Jan. 10 - Nov. 10, 2021. Fully Implemented; Management adhered to the recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous year's recommendations that the CAD, in coordination with the other concerned departments, particularly the CTO, GSD and CED, continue to exert effort in the retrieval of records/reports from respective files that would establish the details of the transactions that took place prior to CY 2003 in order to reclassify the unreconciled beginning balances’ to the appropriate SL accounts.
observations.managementAction: Partially implemented; The majority of the beginning balances of various assets were already reclassified and adjusted.
observations.reason: Non-availab ility of year 2003 & Prior Year's documents/reports to serve as bases for accounts reclassification.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that: The City Accountant – Reconcile the records of bank accounts with the summary of existing accounts maintained by the CTO; Make the necessary adjusting entries to reflect in the books the correct number of bank accounts and the balances of the Cash in Bank Account; and Management requires continuous coordination between the CAD and CTO in reconciling the records of existing bank accounts to assure proper presentation of account balances in the City's financial records.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented; The CTO has submitted the summary list of existing bank accounts for reconciliation. Partially Implemented; For reconciliation. Management requires continuous coordination between the CAD and CTO in reconciling the records of existing bank accounts to assure proper presentation of account balances in the City's financial records.
observations.reason: CTO's submitted data on existing bank accounts are inadequate for reconciliat ion purposes. Analysis of the bank accounts is still ongoing, and the adjusting entries will be based on the results of the analysis and reconciliat ion. The Management is still in the process of reconciliat ion.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous year's recommendations that the City Accountant: Make the necessary adjusting entries to reflect in the books the correct balances of affected asset accounts and submit to this office the JEVs for audit and verification; and Closely monitor the recording of financial transactions and strengthen the review of JEVs and GLs to ensure that all financial transactions are properly and accurately taken up in the books to prevent the occurrence of negative balances in the accounts.
observations.managementAction: Partially implemented; Abnormal balances LEE accounts are partially adjusted. The Management continuously seeks guidance and assistance from COA in strengthening its internal control relative to recording financial transactions.
observations.reason: Analysis of the accounts is ongoing since it involves transactions starting from CY 2011.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management: Maintain a separate depository account for each fund and properly classify the fund intended for the SATF in conformity with Sec 3 of COA Circular No. 92-382; and Analyze the cause of the negative balance in the Quezon Memorial Circle - SATF and prepare the necessary adjusting entries to reflect the correct balance of the account in the books.
observations.managementAction: Partially Implemented; With partial compliance with the recommendation. Fully Implemented; Adjusted in JEV-2021-03-000685 and JEV-2021-03-000686.
observations.reason: There is still one bank account in TF commingled with General Fund.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management require the various recipient NGOs for the immediate settlement/liquidation of the fund transferred to them pursuant to COA Circular No. 2007-001 dated October 25, 2007.

observations.managementAction: Fully Implemented; The City has already submitted the revised Deployment Plan thru COA’s official email last April 5, 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Treasurer exercise the visitorial function as provided under Section 222 of the Revenue Code on big and complicated businesses to check the correctness of taxes paid. The provision authorizes the examination of the books of accounts and pertinent records of businesses to ascertain, assess, and collect the correct amount of tax, fee, or charge. We also recommended the outsourcing of expertise, as necessary, with due consideration of the materiality of evidence at hand and the expected return for the City Government.
observations.managementAction: Partially implemented; The City Treasurer drafted a letter to issue a Letter of Authority (LOA) to exercise the visitorial function as provided in the Code.
observations.reason: Due to manpower constraints and the COVID-19 pandemic, examination of the business books of accounts is put on hold.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Treasurer exercise the visitorial function as provided under Section 222 of the Revenue Code on these businesses to check on the accuracy of the assessment and collection of taxes, in view of the substantial differences in the declared gross receipts against the selling transactions disclosed by the audit of DOAS.
observations.managementAction: Partially Implemented; The system was already deployed. Examination of the books of accounts is ongoing.
observations.reason: Lack of manpower to carry out the visitorial function and the COVID-19 pandemic.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the CTO propose to the Sangguniang Panlalawigan the amendment of the provision on Contractor’s Tax as to the mathematical formula for tax bases above P2,000,000.00 similar to then City Revenue Code.
observations.managementAction: Partially Implemented; Management created a review committee to study the City’s Revenue Code amendments.
observations.reason: Still in the process of reviewing the recommendation. Thus, there is no proposed amendment draft to the Contractors’ Tax.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management fast-track the project on the construction of a centralized slaughterhouse in order that the City can ensure adequate and safe food supply to all residents as envisioned in its Comprehensive Development Plan. The centralized slaughterhouse will, likewise, settle issues pertaining to abattoirs operating near or within residential and commercial areas and/or inability to secure franchise and Special Use Permit.
observations.managementAction: Partially Implemented; Ongoing coordination between the Payatas Controlled Disposal Facility (PCDF) Technical Working Group (TWG, headed by the Office of the City Administrator) and DENR-EMB to align the City’s initiative to build Quezon City-owned and operated Double-A Abattoir or Slaughterhouse. CED ensures site suitability, a slope stabilization concept, a conceptual plan and a Detailed Engineering Design (DED) for the project.
observations.reason: The City is yet to construct a centralized slaughterhouse.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the lump-sum appropriations provided in the annual budgets for CY 2015 and 2014 be required with the necessary SP approval prior to the implementation of the above projects in the total amount of P574,981,803.17 in compliance with the provision of the law and following the ruling provided in the afore-cited jurisprudence.
observations.managementAction: Partially Implemented; Submitted Sangguniang Panlungsod (SP) authorization in some of the implemented projects.
observations.reason: No additional SP authorizati ons were submitted by management.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the management: Submit/present the stock certificates and/or explanation on the non-presentation of stock certificates for the investments on Meralco stocks of P12,001,517.93 under SEF; and Require the CAD in coordination with the CTO to verify if the Meralco stocks in the amount of P1,963,220.00 under GF were already redeemed and record the said transaction/effect adjustment in the books.
observations.managementAction: Partially Implemented; These were verified as bill deposits to Meralco to install extension lines and other power facilities to various schools in Quezon City. Of the total amount of P1,963,220.00, the amount of Php 1,165,510.00 was already verified to have certificates of stocks, and these are not yet redeemed.
observations.reason: Stock Certificate s are not available. Only the document from the CTO indicating the Serial Number of the Stocks was submitted. Pertinent documents were not available.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that concerned Management officials revisit the provisions of the Revenue Code to come up with possible amendments that would redound to the best interest of the City and the citizenry without going beyond the provisions and requirements of the LGC and its IRR. Moreover, to fully address the revenue concerns, we have also suggested that concerned officials gather information on the new and emerging businesses, professions, or occupations for possible updating of the City Code after a series of public consultations and hearings.
observations.managementAction: Partially Implemented; Review Committee was created by management to study and recommend amendments to the City’s Revenue Code.
observations.reason: Still in the process of reviewing the Revenue Code.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management amend City Resolution No. SP-2111, S-2003 dated July 1, 2003, to conform with the definition and utilization of idle funds, as prescribed under COA Circular No. 92-382 dated July 3, 1992, and reiterated under Section 109, Volume I of the NGAS Manual.
observations.managementAction: Fully Implemented; All active Time Deposits from the TFs were already terminated.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that:

Management strictly follow the rules in granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advance as prescribed in COA Circular No. 97-002.
Concerned officers and employees immediately settle their unliquidated cash advances.
The Municipal Accountant issue a certification that the previous cash advance has been liquidated and accounted for in the books before granting additional cash advances.
The Municipal Accountant should be careful in choosing account titles to be used in their Financial Statement. He should follow the account titles prescribed in Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-09.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant already issued Demand Letters to the officials and employees with unliquidated cash advances and submitted their documents. Some cash advances were liquidated and old accounts are gradually deducted from salaries.
observations.reason: As of December 31, 2021, the Municipality still have an outstanding unliquidated cash advances amounting to ₱706,241.61.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management through the concerned Department Heads implement the following:

The Disbursing Officer fully liquidate his/her cash advances for operating expenses in order that the corresponding amount of expenses shall be recorded in the books of the Municipality.  Likewise, demand from the officials and employees with unliquidated cash advances to submit their liquidation documents or make refunds to fully settle their outstanding accounts, otherwise, the amounts shall be deducted from any amount due them or worse, they may be charged with malversation of public funds.
The Municipal Accountant monitor closely the liquidation of Cash Advances granted to Accountable Officers/Officers and Employees within the prescribed period to ensure prompt settlement of accounts. The no liquidation no cash advance policy must be strictly followed.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant already issued Demand Letters to the officials and employees with unliquidated cash advances and submitted their documents. Some cash advances were liquidated and old accounts are gradually deducted from salaries.
observations.reason: As of December 31, 2021, the Municipality still have an outstanding unliquidated cash advances amounting to ₱706,241.61

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management prioritize the production of the Electrical Plan required by ISELCO for the immediate installation of the additional transformer necessary for the operation of said X-ray machine. We further recommended that the Municipal Planning and Development Officer adopt proper planning including all the necessary measures before any procurement to ensure that anything procured would be immediately utilized.

Likewise, stop the practice of leaving huge amount of cash in their possession to avoid possible loss through misuse or theft/robbery of government funds.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management establish specific guidelines in the granting of financial assistance for uniformity and fairness. observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the 

(a) designated officer responsible for the receipt and inventory of donations in-kind to
 (i) comply with Section II.1 (a) of the COA Circular No. 2020-09 by accounting all  donations in-kind received during quarantine period; and 



(ii) to submit all the required supporting documents of the donations in-kind received from all  the donors such as the acknowledgement receipt and inventory report; and 

(b) OIC-Municipal Accountant to make the necessary accounting entries to correct the recording of receipt of cash donations and the necessary closing entries at year end or upon completion of its purpose, whichever comes first, pursuant to Section IV (B.3 and B.13) of the COA Circular 2014-02.
observations.managementAction:
The Management submitted the lacking supporting documents as the acknowledgement receipt and inventory report.

The necessary accounting entries were already made by the Accounting Office.
observations.reason: The Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances was not submitted for the donation in-kind received by the Municipality in CY 2021.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Management agreed to continue complying with the BIR rules and regulations on the withholding and remittance of withheld taxes on compensation and value added taxes, percentage taxes and expanded taxes from government money payments to ensure funds for the implementation of various government programs and projects.  

However, the Accounting Unit should investigate the occurrence of negative balance in Due to BIR account under SEF and Trust Fund and make the adjustment in the succeeding remittance to BIR if necessary.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality remitted the total amount of ₱9,092,425.54 representing various taxes withheld for CY 2021, thus leaving a taxes payable of only ₱100,785.22 representing taxes withheld for the month of December 2021.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the management agreed to

 (a) continue complying with the GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG regulations on the withholding and remittance of employees’ and employer contributions and loan amortizations of employees to the respective agencies; and 

 (b) exert effort in identifying the year-end balances particularly those accumulated during the year and cause remittance, if necessary
observations.managementAction: The Municipality substantially complied with the withholding and remittance of premiums, contribution and loan amortizations for GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG regulations for CY 2021.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management comply strictly with COA Circular No. 2009-001 and COA Circular No. 94-006 such as:

1.Furnish the Auditor copy of contract (including purchase orders and memorandum of agreement) and each of all the documents forming part thereof by reference of incorporation within five (5) working days from the execution of contract; and

2.Notify the Auditor within 24 hours from accepting deliveries of procured items.
observations.managementAction: The agency failed to submit copies of perfected contracts, purchase orders, memorandum of agreements and supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor within five (5) working days from the execution of a contract or issuance thereof

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management strictly observe the General Provision of General Appropriations Act for FY 2019 which provides a limitation on the net take home pay of officials and employees of the government in the amount not lower than P5,000.00.
observations.managementAction: Management still fail to observe the General Provision of General Appropriations Act for FY 2020 which provides a limitation on the net take home pay of officials and employees

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that management:

ensure that trust funds are used exclusively to the purpose/s for which they were granted following the provisions of Section 4(3) of P.D. No. 1445 and Sections 305(e) and 309(b) of R.A. No. 7160.

require the settlement of long outstanding receivables to augment the deficient cash under trust fund.
observations.managementAction: Management failed to comply with the audit recommendation on the enforcement of long outstanding receivables and the insufficiency of cash backup was not addressed.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 10, page 45, Part II of this AAR.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that management: 

a.	Exert diligent effort on the liquidation of the receivables thru demand to effect immediate settlement of the account and send tracers if not acted upon; and

b.	Advise the Accountant to effect the adjustments on the related accounts and to strictly adhere to existing applicable accounting and auditing rules and regulations to ensure fair presentation of financial statements.
observations.managementAction: No management action.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 16, page 58, Part II of this AAR.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:

analyze the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts and trace back the differences by determining the nature of the accounts;

prepare the necessary adjusting entries to bring the correct balances of the accounts; and

maintain subsidiary ledger as necessary and include disclosure in the Notes to FS for fair presentation of Financial Statements
observations.managementAction: No management action.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 9, page 44, Part II of this AAR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Accountant to properly monitor and reconcile the balance of receivables on disallowances/charges recognized in the financial statements with that contained in COA reports and make the necessary adjustments.  Henceforth, proof of settlements should be simultaneously presented to the office of the auditor for recording and timely issuance of Notice of Settlement of Suspension/ Disallowance/ Charge (NSSDC).
observations.managementAction: No management action.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 22, page 73, Part II of this AAR.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that management:

Create an Inventory Committee to undertake the physical count of inventory items as of June 30 and December 31 of each year; to prepare the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RCPI) by inventory account name and distribute the same as prescribed; to reconcile said report with the records of the Accounting Office in accordance with Section 124, Volume I of MNGAS; and to submit to the Office of the Auditor the duplicate copy of the RCPI not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the ﬁrst and second semester, respectively;

Direct the Municipal Accountant to correct the errors in recording purchases and issuances of inventories and observe the Perpetual Inventory System as method of recording inventories as prescribed under Section 51, Volume I of the MNGAS for LGUS; and 

Instruct the General Services Officer (GSO) and the Municipal Accountant to maintain updated Stock Cards and Supplies Ledger Cards and regularly reconcile records to detect discrepancies and effect immediate corrections; and for the GSO to promptly prepare and submit the monthly RSMIs to the Municipal Accountant for recording in compliance with Sections 119 and 120, Volume I, of the MNGAS for LGUs..
observations.managementAction: No management action.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 13, page 52, Part II of this AAR.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to: a. revert those account whose owners/creditors replied but was not able to submit, upon request, the required legal documents pertaining to their claim and secure from the Sangguniang Bayan (SB) a resolution with a condition that appropriation shall be made in the event that they were able to provide those documents; and b. continue to exert more effort to locate other owners/creditors and confirm the account and request them to submit documents pertaining to their claims with the Municipality. If creditors will not be able to support their claims, revert the account to the Unappropriated Surplus of the GF, subject to an authority from the SB through a Resolution with a condition that appropriation shall be made in the event that a claim arises in the future pursuant to the provisions of Section 98 of PD No. 1445.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality will go through the process in the early part of the following year.
observations.reason: No action has yet been taken as the Municipal Accountant will start the process in the ensuing year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the (a) Acting Municipal Treasurer and OIC - Municipal Accountant to reconcile their records at least quarterly to immediately detect any error/discrepancy for timely adjustment and accordingly, to establish an accurate and reliable records and reports on Cash in Treasury and Cash in Bank balances; and (b) OIC - Municipal Accountant to (i) investigate the existence and the nature of bank account with long outstanding subsidiary balances with zero bank balances; (ii) prepare Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS) for the remaining cash in three (3) bank accounts; (iii) provide sufficient details on the reconciling items in the BRS; and (iv) trace and investigate the dormant account recorded in the books at ₱(27,927.73) with outstanding bank balance of ₱32,320.78  and make the necessary adjustments thereof.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the (a) Acting Municipal Treasurer and OIC-Municipal Accountant to reconcile their records of Real Property Tax (RPT) receivables in order to report reliably the said account in the financial statements; and (b) Acting Municipal Treasurer to develop additional  strategies that would intensify the collections of RPT which includes conduct of frequent tax campaigns and posting of Notice of Delinquency.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the (a) Inventory Committee to (i) conduct the physical inventory of all PPE of the Municipality and immediately submit related reports to the Office of the Auditor; (ii) reconcile the inventory report/results with the Accounting records to ensure fair presentation of the accounts; and (b) OIC-Municipal Accountant and the designated property officer to maintain complete PPE records such as subsidiary ledger cards for each group of PPEs, property cards, Property Acknowledgement Receipts and inventory tags for proper monitoring and control of the agency properties.

The Accounting Office commented that they will trace back the records to reconcile the difference of ₱1,997,034.18 between the reported balance of unexpended DRMMF from previous years and Accounting Office’s report.

We recommended and the Management agreed to continue its effort to prevent cash deficit through introduction of cost-cutting measures and limiting the expenditures to the essential operating needs of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to submit immediately the unsubmitted disbursement voucher including its supporting documents.  Henceforth, strictly comply with the submission within the 10th day of the following month as stated in COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the BAC Chairman and Property Officer to strictly comply with the submission of contracts, purchase orders and all the documents forming part thereof within five (5) working days from its execution and/or issuance as provided by COA Circular No. 2009-001.

We reiterated our prior years’ recommendation that the Local Chief Executive require the OIC - Municipal Accountant to investigate and make necessary adjustments on the variance of the two reciprocal accounts if needed, and see it to it that all future transactions in the said accounts are properly recorded.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive through the Municipal Development Council and Municipal Planning and Development Office agreed to refrain from including expenditure items that are not allowed to be charged against the Development Fund (DF).

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive and the Local School Board (LSB) agreed to (a) continue complying with the utilization and prioritization of SEF projects accordingly; (b) instruct the Inventory Committee to conduct physical inventory of all properties booked under SEF and prepare a Report thereon; and (c) require the Accounting Office and the Property Officer to properly maintain subsidiary ledger cards and property cards for all assets procured out of SEF.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the LDRRM Officer to (a) prepare the annual LDRRM Investment Plan and cause its submission to Office of the Civil Defense and DILG; and (b) cause the submission of the monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF to COA Auditor as required by COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the (a) Bids and Awards Committee; Head, BAC Secretariat and Technical Working Group to work on the completion of the documentary deficiencies and submit the same to the Audit Team; and (b) Municipal Accountant to ensure that the required documents for each type of transaction are submitted/attached to the disbursement vouchers before the processing and payments.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the (a) BAC to adhere to the provisions of Section 4 (6) of PD No. 1445 and Government Procurement Policy Board (GPBB) Circular No. 01-2020 dated April 6, 2020 on the documentary requirements for emergency procurement; (b) OIC-Municipal Accountant to comply with the BIR rules and regulations on the withholding and remittance of withholding taxes on value added taxes, percentage taxes and expanded taxes from government money payments; and (c) BAC and OIC-Municipal Accountant to submit all the lacking documents such as the Mayor’s or Business Permit, Income Tax Return and Omnibus Sworn Statement.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the (a) designated officer responsible for the receipt and inventory of donations in-kind to (i) comply with Section II.1 (a) of the COA Circular No. 2020-09 by accounting all  donations in-kind received during quarantine period; and (ii) to submit all the required supporting documents of the donations in-kind received from all  the donors such as the acknowledgement receipt and inventory report; and (b) OIC-Municipal Accountant to make the necessary accounting entries to correct the recording of receipt of cash donations and the necessary closing entries at year end or upon completion of its purpose, whichever comes first, pursuant to Section IV (B.3 and B.13) of the COA Circular 2014-02.

We recommended that the LCE (a) make sure the Annual GPB is at least 5% of the total annual budget appropriations; (b) ensure the implementation of GAD programs and activities in accordance with approved GAD plans and budget; and (c) instruct the GAD Focal Point Person to use the Harmonized Gender and Development Guidelines (HGDG) in the attribution of costs of regular program/project to GAD.

We recommended and the Management agreed to continue complying with the BIR rules and regulations on the withholding and remittance of withheld taxes on compensation and value added taxes, percentage taxes and expanded taxes from government money payments to ensure funds for the implementation of various government programs and projects.  However, the Accounting Unit should investigate the occurrence of negative balance in Due to BIR account under SEF and Trust Fund and make the adjustment in the succeeding remittance to BIR if necessary.

We recommended and the management agreed to (a) continue complying with the GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG regulations on the withholding and remittance of employees’ and employer contributions and loan amortizations of employees to the respective agencies; and exert effort in identifying the year-end balances particularly those accumulated during the year and cause remittance, if necessary

We recommended that:
Management strictly follow the rules in granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advance as prescribed in COA Circular No. 97-002.
Concerned officers and employees immediately settle their unliquidated cash advances.
The Municipal Accountant issue a certification that the previous cash advance has been liquidated and accounted for in the books before granting additional cash advances.
The Municipal Accountant should be careful in choosing account titles to be used in their Financial Statement. He should follow the account titles prescribed in Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-09.

We recommended that the Management through the concerned Department Heads implement the following:
The Disbursing Officer fully liquidate his/her cash advances for operating expenses in order that the corresponding amount of expenses shall be recorded in the books of the Municipality.  Likewise, demand from the officials and employees with unliquidated cash advances to submit their liquidation documents or make refunds to fully settle their outstanding accounts, otherwise, the amounts shall be deducted from any amount due them or worse, they may be charged with malversation of public funds.
The Municipal Accountant monitor closely the liquidation of Cash Advances granted to Accountable Officers/Officers and Employees within the prescribed period to ensure prompt settlement of accounts. The no liquidation no cash advance policy must be strictly followed.

We recommended that the Management prioritize the production of the Electrical Plan required by ISELCO for the immediate installation of the additional transformer necessary for the operation of said X-ray machine. We further recommended that the Municipal Planning and Development Officer adopt proper planning including all the necessary measures before any procurement to ensure that anything procured would be immediately utilized.

We recommended that the Management through the Municipal Treasurer and Assistant Municipal Treasurer deposit regularly and intact the full amount of income collection in strict compliance with Section 69 of PD 1445 and Section 33 and Section 66, Volume I of Government Accounting Manual (GAM) for Local Government. Likewise, stop the practice of leaving huge amount of cash in their possession to avoid possible loss through misuse or theft/robbery of government funds.

We recommended that the Management through the MGSO prepare an updated physical inventory report of all its properties. We also recommended that the MGSO/Property Custodian maintain property records and furnish copy thereof to the Municipal Accountant. Both records must be reconciled regularly.

We recommended that the Management through the Municipal Accountant submit the necessary reports on timely basis and strictly comply with the provisions in COA Circular No 2009-006 and PD 1445 in order to avoid imposition of the sanctions provided therein.

We recommended that the Management through the Municipal Engineer and Municipal Planning Development Coordinator submit copies of the following documents to support the payments:
Approved Program of Works for the Barangay road concreting projects.
Provision of the allocation for DRRM activities in the approved annual/supplemental budgets
Approved SB Resolution for the realignment/augmentation of budget in accordance with Section 136 of RA 7160
Approved Supplemental Development Plan (DP) and Annual Investment Plan (AIP) for the changes/realignment of the 20% Development Fund
Daily Equipment Utilization Report (with Hours Rendered and Works Accomplished indicated therein).

Management Comment:
Management agreed with our recommendation and will do their best through the Municipal Engineer and Municipal Planning Development Coordinator to submit copies of the necessary supporting documents for every projects, programs and activities of the municipality.

We recommended that the Management through the OIC-Municipal Engineer and the Project Engineer require the contractors of said projects to make the necessary repairs to the above defective projects at their own expenses, otherwise, the amount corresponding to the deficiency shall be disallowed in audit.
We recommended that the Management through the Municipal Accountant maintain subsidiary ledgers so as to provide historical data for the controlling accounts in the general ledger.

We recommended that the Management comply strictly with COA Circular Nos. 2009-001 and 94-006 and:
1.  Furnish the Auditor copies of contracts (including purchase orders and memorandum of agreement) and each of all the documents forming part thereof by reference of incorporation within five (5) working days from the execution of contract; and
2. Notify the Auditor within 24 hours from accepting deliveries of procured items.

We recommended that Management through the OIC-Municipal Engineer and Project Manager / In-Charge implement an efficient monitoring system for all on-going projects. We further recommend that Management impose the liquidated damages amounting P10,362.56 and require the contractor to pay the full amount immediately.

We recommended that the Management strictly observe the General Provision of General Appropriations Act for FY 2020 which provides a limitation on the net take home pay of officials and employees of the government in the amount not lower than P5,000.00.

We recommended that Management establish specific guidelines in the granting of financial assistance for uniformity and fairness. Likewise, the members of the Sangguniang Bayan should stop the practice of giving financial assistance to its constituents regardless of source of fund as it is not among their mandated powers, duties and functions under Section 447 of RA 7160 as well as to avoid possible misuse of government funds.
We recommended that Management comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 on the reportorial requirements.

We recommended that Management submit copies of the following documents to support the payments:
Approved budget for the bayanihan grant releases consistent with Local Budget Circular (LBC) 125
List of LSIs served with meals and snacks
Provision of the allocation for DRRM activities in the approved annual/supplemental budgets
Approval of ordinances for realignment/augmentation of budget in accordance with Section 136 of RA 7160
Updated Supplemental Development Plan (DP) and Annual Investment Plan (AIP) for the changes/realignment of the 20% Development Fund
Updated Supplemental Development Plan (DP) and Annual Investment Plan (AIP) for the changes/realignment of the 20% Development Fund.
Payments of wages that was improperly charged against the COVID 19 Funds be transferred to the General Fund Proper to recompense the COVID 19 Fund for its intended purposes/use, otherwise the amount shall be disallowed in audit.
Management should also ensure that succeeding procurements made through alternative mode should be properly documented.

Management Comment:
Management agreed to our observation and will comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002 on the reportorial requirements and ensure that succeeding procurements made through alternative mode will be properly documented.
Henceforth, we recommended that management sustain the same efforts in its future undertakings.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
in coordination with the BAC Chairman, submit immediately the lacking documentary requirements for verification; and
ensure that all documentary requirements prescribed by regulations are complied with before certifying as to completeness of the supporting documents of the disbursement vouchers.

We recommended that management ensure that all the relevant and required information in Purchase Orders are properly accomplished to ensure that the supplier will deliver the goods/items in accordance with the terms and conditions of the contract.
We recommended that management require the submission of the lacking supporting documents for further review by this office.

We recommended that management:
ensure the allocation and utilization of LDRRMF pursuant to the policies and guidelines primarily embodied in NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1; and
thru the LDRRMC, continuously monitor, coordinate and evaluate PPAs to ensure the completion of most urgent, needed, appropriate and eligible projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
prepare the necessary adjusting entry to the affected accounts to ensure fair presentation of financial statements;
transfer unexpended balance of DRRMF other than those appropriated for capital outlays to the Trust Fund and recognized as Trust Liabilities – DRRMF; and
abide with the guidelines set forth under Items 5.1.10 and 5.1.11 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

We recommended that:
the Accounting Office comply with the provision Section 114 of P.D. No 1445 and Section 4 (e) of the Government Accounting Manual for LGUs, Volume I, so that all transactions would be properly documented and recorded. With this, all transactions can be readily verified and reconciled with the amount appearing in the financial statements. Thus, the accuracy and reliability of the financial statements will be achieved; and
management consider hiring more personnel to augment the staff requirements of the Accounting Office.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant thoroughly review the transactions which resulted in the negative balances of accounts and accordingly prepare adjustments to establish the accuracy and valuation thereof.

We recommended and reiterated that the Municipal Accountant:
analyze the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts, trace back differences and the nature of the accounts;
prepare the necessary adjusting entries to bring the correct balances of the accounts; and
maintain subsidiary ledger as necessary and include disclosure in the Notes to FS for fair presentation of Financial Statements

We recommended that management ensure that trust funds are used exclusively to the purpose/s for which they were granted following the provisions of Section 4(3) of P.D. No. 1445 and Sections 305(e) and 309(b) of R.A. No. 7160.

We recommended that management require:
the complete physical inventory of municipal properties and immediately reconcile the balances of any affected accounts to permit fair presentation of PPE in the financial statements;
the maintenance of PPE Subsidiary Ledger Cards and Property Cards to properly account the receipt and disposition of PPE items; and
disclosures for the Land Account in the Notes to Financial Statements

We recommended that management require:
the Municipal Engineer to provide the Accountant and the General Service Officer with the complete description and segregation of road components for those with data on construction cost. For those not posted in the registry but without identified cost and those included in the RPCRNS but not in the registry, the fair value must be used in determining the cost of road components and thereafter shall provide the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects;
the Municipal Accountant to depreciate the carrying amount of existing roads and its components to reflect the asset in its used condition and accordingly adjust the amount recorded in the Road Network account supported by the RPCRNS, Registry Infrastructure for Roads and working paper on the determination of the depreciated replacement cost per registry. Subsequently, at the end of each year, a lapsing schedule for the computation of depreciation for each depreciable road component must be prepared as basis for recording depreciation expenses and the required Local Road Network Ledger Card for each road network shall likewise be maintained; and
the MGSO at the end or every accounting period to render a Report on Local Road Network using the pro-forma schedule in Annex A of same Circular and maintain local road inventory, Road Map and complete Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components.

We recommended that management:
instruct the Inventory Committee to undertake the physical count of inventory items as of June 30 and December 31 of each year; to prepare the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RCPI) by inventory account name and distribute the same as prescribed; to reconcile said report with the records of the Accounting Office in accordance with Section 194, Volume I of GAM; and to submit to the Office of the Auditor the duplicate copy of the RCPI not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the ﬁrst and second semester, respectively; and
direct the Municipal Accountant to correct the errors in recording purchases and issuances of inventories and observe the Perpetual Inventory System as method of recording inventories as prescribed under Section 182, Volume I of the GAM for LGUS.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to update and provide depreciation to the affected PPE account in order to present their reasonable book values in the financial statements in conformity with the provisions of IPSAS 17 and GAM.
The audit team further recommended that the accountant provide an adjusting entry to reclassify the Sports Equipment to appropriate Inventory Accounts. Likewise, if the items were already issued to end users, adjust the same to Prior Period Adjustment account and require the accomplishment of Inventory Custodian Slips to establish accountability on the items.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of the receivables and determine the concerned debtors to ascertain their collectability. If proven to be no longer collectible, request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts and attach required documents prescribed under COA Circular No. 2016-005; and
should maintain subsidiary ledgers in accordance with Section 4 (e) of the NGAS to ensure complete information on the accounts and to facilitate the collection thereof.

We recommended and reiterated that management:
exert diligent effort on the liquidation of the receivables thru demand to effect immediate settlement of the account and send tracers if not acted upon; and
advise the Accountant to effect the adjustments on the related accounts and to strictly adhere to existing applicable accounting and auditing rules and regulations for fair presentation of financial statements.

We recommended that management:
require/demand the immediate settlement of all long overdue cash advances and if necessary, withhold the salaries of the concerned officials and employees for the settlement thereof; and
strictly comply with the provisions of Section 89 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 97 – 002 dated February 10, 1997 in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.

We recommended that the Local School Board, being the one responsible in the formulation, approval, and utilization of the SEF Budget as provided under R.A. No. 7160 or the Local Government Code:
sustain its efforts in ensuring that all budgeted projects, programs and activities are optimally and fully implemented;
consider realignment of funds to other priority programs, projects and activities, if warranted by circumstances; and
from time to time, revisit the revised guidelines on the use of SEF under DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017 to ensure a more strategic and efficient formulation and utilization of resources for priority programs and projects.

We recommended that management sustain its efforts and commitment towards full compliance with standards and requirements set under R.A. No. 9003. To this end, sufficient appropriation should be provided to ensure its full and immediate implementation.

We recommended the following:
The Local Chief Executive and concerned municipal officials must ensure that at least 5% of the annual budget of the municipality can be attributed and utilized on GAD-related programs, projects and activities;
The members of the GAD Focal Point System to undergo trainings on HGDG to strengthen their knowledge and capabilities in the conduct of gender analysis of programs and projects for purposes of GAD budget attribution; and
The members of the GAD Focal Point System to use monitoring tools such as the HGDG and PIMME in the GAD planning and budgeting as well as in the preparation of the accomplishment report.

We recommended:
the Local Chief Executive to enforce the settlement of the above audit disallowances which have become final and executory and in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-006;
the Municipal Accountant to disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the disallowances which have not yet become final and executory; and
the Municipal Treasurer to immediately notify the Audit Team regarding settlement of disallowances and submit proof of settlement thereof for prompt issuance of NSSDC.

We recommended that the Management require the Disbursing Officer and the Accountant to reconcile their cashbook and accounting records, respectively, at least quarterly in compliance to Section 96 of the Government Accounting Manual for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:
submit a written explanation for the incurrence of overage. Otherwise, the amount shall be forfeited in favor of the government pursuant to Section 101 of the Government Accounting Manual for Local Government Units; and
properly monitor her accountabilities and observe proper safekeeping of collections by separating personal monies from municipal funds and comply with the provision of Section 101(1) of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that the Petty Cash Fund be maintained under the imprest system in accordance to Section 84 of GAM, Volume I.

We recommended that the Accountable Officer prepare the monthly Report on Accountability for Accountable Forms (RAAF) at the end of each month in compliance with the above-cited regulation.

We recommended that the Management require the Municipal Treasurer and the Accountant to reconcile their cashbook and accounting records, respectively, at least quarterly in compliance to Section 96 of the Government Accounting Manual for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that management continue the practice of depositing collections regularly to comply with the rules and regulations as mandated by Section 69 of P.D. No. 1445 and Section 32 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I on collections and deposits.
We recommended that the liquidating officer maintain/retain a permanent file of RCDs of collectors to facilitate the verification and reconciliation of individual accountabilities.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer require all collectors to render their RAAF at the end of each month. Henceforth, these individual RAAFs should be reconciled with the Consolidated RAAF to ensure its accuracy.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the General Services Officer to
determine the value of all the PPE items in the RPCPPE and present the said report
per type of PPE as required in the MNGAS.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Agriculturist to
