observations.recommendation: We recommended to direct the Municipal Accountant to reissue demand letters and exhaust all possible remedies to collect the receivables and to demand liquidation of cash advances and fund transfers. Conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of these accounts. Comply with all the conditions and requirements of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 for the possible request for the write off of the specified dormant receivables and cash advances accounts to the Commission on Audit. Provide allowance for impairment thereafter and reconcile the accounts with unidentified/ unreconciled subsidiary ledgers from prior years in order to have a fair presentation of affected accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Reissued demand letter to accountable Officers for the liquidation of the accounts. Ongoing reconciliation and analysis of the unidentified accounts. Request for write off will be made after reconciliation of receivable accounts.
observations.reason: Unwillingness of accountable officer to settle the cash advances and resignation of some accountable officers. Accounting employees are still on the process of verifying the available accounting records for all unidentified receivable accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended management that financial assistance be properly handled by the Municipal Social Welfare Office for proper assessment of each case and the appropriateness of the documents presented vis-à-vis the requirements. A case study shall then be prepared as basis for payment.
observations.managementAction:
All financial assistance were handled to MSWDO with proper assessment per DSWD Cir. No. 2 of 2014 and DSWD MC No. 11 s. 2019.

MSWD prepared Certificate of eligibility or case study as supporting documents to all AICS vouchers.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to require the Bids and Awards Committee to include in its schedule of activities the procurement of fuel, oil and lubricants to be procured through public bidding. Publish the bid in the Phil-GEPS website to promote transparency and competitiveness to notify possible interested bidders who can afford to provide the most advantageous contract price, discounts and additional service offers. 

The Municipal Accountant should also assure that disbursement vouchers are supported with adequate documents to avoid disallowance or suspension of transactions. 
The Municipal Mayor and the Municipal Accountant should see to it that authorized passengers are indicated in the vehicle trip tickets, including the purpose of travel, to establish validity of request for gasoline and to make accountable those personnel who claims fare and gasoline at the same time, thus to avoid possible abuse on these transactions.
observations.managementAction: Bids and Awards Committee thru the Head of Procuring Entity (HOPE) implemented the procurement of fuel, oil and lubricants for 2019 through Public Bidding

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer to be more prudent in the grant of honoraria taking into consideration the limitations provided under Budget Circular 2007-1 and 2007-2.

Assign an individual to review activity design for training and determine what is most advantageous to the municipality.  

Direct all persons liable to cause the refund of the honoraria/incentive in the total amount of P6,922,617.01 received by the municipal officials and employees of the municipality and other designated members of various committees.
observations.managementAction: The giving of honoraria and incentive were stopped to those members of the committees especially if it is within his official functions. Program/ Activity design were properly reviewed and analyzed before the approval by the LFC.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous year’s recommendations that the City Accounting Department (CAD), in coordination with the other concerned departments, particularly the City Treasurer’s Office (CTO), General Services Department (GSD) and the Engineering Department continue to exert effort in the retrieval of records/reports from respective files that would establish the details of the transactions that took before CY 2003 in order to reclassify the “unreconciled beginning balances’ to the appropriate SL accounts.

We recommended that Management:
Coordinate with the depository bank for the prompt release of the monthly statements to facilitate the timely preparation of the BRS;
Prepare and submit the BRS for the other accounts; and
Henceforth, comply strictly the requirements of Section 3.2 of COA Circular No. 96-011 to facilitate the timely review of the BRS.

We recommended that Management:
Submit the unrecorded DVs/Payrolls as well as Reports of Collection together with the corresponding Official Receipts through the CTO to the CAD for recording and adjust the balances of the CIB-LCCA and Government Equity accounts as of year-end by P44,785,872.31; and
Henceforth, always record reconciling items in the BRS to fairly present year-end balances.

We recommended that Management increase efforts in strengthening the controls in the grant, liquidation and monitoring of cash advances by:
Instructing the concerned officers and employees to immediately liquidate their outstanding cash advances;
Requiring the City Accountant to strictly monitor the liquidation of cash advances by sending demand letters regularly, particularly to accountable officers and employees who are still in active service, and in case of failure to liquidate the same despite demand, withholding of the payment of any money due them may be made pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002; and
Evaluating individually the probability of collection and consider request for write-off pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated 
December 19, 2016, for the unliquidated cash advances granted to the retired, resigned or inactive and deceased employees.

We recommended that Management:
Reconcile the records of the GSD and the CAD as regards the demolished building and other structures;
Require the CAD to prepare JEV to record the derecognition, copy furnished the Office of the Auditor for evaluation and reference; and
Henceforth, properly coordinate with the CAD, Engineering and General Services for proper documentation and recording of demolished buildings and other structures.

We recommended and the Management agreed to:
Make the necessary adjusting entries to reflect in the books the correct balances of affected asset and liability accounts as discussed in Paragraph 2.2 and submit to the Office of the City Auditor the JEVs for audit and verification; and
Closely monitor the recording of financial transactions and strengthen review of JEVs and GLs to ensure that all financial transactions are properly and accurately taken up in the books and to prevent the occurrence of negative balances in the accounts.

We recommended that Management:
Stop the practice of charging to the SEF the payment of salaries and other personnel benefits to non-teaching personnel of City schools; and
Refrain from hiring job order personnel whose duties and responsibilities duplicate those of outsourced services such as janitorial and security personnel.

We recommended that Management:
Submit immediately the lacking documents to the Office of the Auditor for verification and audit;
Stop immediately the practice of processing payments without complete supporting documents; and
Henceforth, comply strictly with Section 9 of COA Circular 
No. 2012-001.

We recommended that Management:
Review thoroughly the grounds for the suspension of work of the 28 infrastructure projects to determine their propriety;
Consider issuing Order to resume work for projects whose grounds for suspension are not covered by Sections 9.1 and 9.2; and
Henceforth, comply strictly the requirements of Sections 9.1 and 9.2 in suspending infrastructure projects.

We recommended that Management:
Ensure the implementation of 100 percent accomplishment of planned activities under the 20% DF considering that funds are available for these activities; and
Henceforth, instruct the Development Fund focal person to conduct regular coordination with the implementing offices to ensure that program/projects/activities are undertaken within the target period.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the concerned City Official to see to it that funds are utilized and expended in accordance with the guidelines on the appropriation and utilization of the 20% DF pursuant to DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated 22 February 2017.

We recommended that Management cause the immediate remittance of the remaining Green Fund maintained by retailers, being a public fund subject to COA audit, in the total amount of P316.598 million as of December 31, 2018 including interest, if any.

We recommended that Management propose the following amendments of the City Ordinance to the Sangguniang Panlungsod:
a.  	Amendments to an existing ordinance:
b. Additional provisions to be included in the Ordinance:
Review mechanism in evaluating the effectiveness of the Ordinance every 5 years or as determined by the task force/City Council;
Transitional provision to gradually ban the use of plastic bags after a period of 6 months or as determined by the task force/City Council; and
Provision for purposes of examining the books of accounts and pertinent records of retailers in order to ascertain, assess, and collect the correct amount of Green Fund by the City Treasurer in accordance with Article 259 of the Implementing Rules and Regulations of R.A. No.7160.

We recommended that Management monitor properly the incentive schemes adopted by relevant retailers to use reusable bags and/or exchange their used plastic bags for new ones in compliance with Section 14 (j) of Ordinance No. SP-2350, S-2014.

We recommended that the Human Resource Management and Development Office (HRMDO), the City CAD, and the CTO:
Formulate standard operating procedure/policy that will oversee early preparation and processing of the disbursement vouchers relative to the remittances of the employees’ monthly premium contributions to the PHIC; and
Henceforth, strictly comply with Section 20(b), Rule III, Title III of the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of the National Health Insurance Act 5 (RA No. 7875 as amended by RA No. 9241).

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Accountant immediately enforce the settlement of all audit suspensions, disallowances and charges in compliance with Sections 5.4 and 7.1.1, Chapter II of COA Circular 
No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009; and Management to be more vigilant in the implementation of the existing accounting and auditing rules and regulations in processing of claims to avoid possible audit suspensions and disallowances.

We recommended the following to the Management:
The Municipal Engineer provide the Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer with the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects.
The General Services Officer maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map and shall keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components.
The Municipal Accountant prepare the Journal Voucher to record the inventory of the local road network and its components in the general ledger and the Local Road Network Ledger Card.  He shall keep and maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for every road network, and ensure that 100% of the value of road network of the Municipality shall be booked-up in 2019 consistent with COA Circular No. 2015-008. Full disclosure on road networks should be made in the Notes to the Financial Statements of the Municipality.
The Municipal Engineer, General Service Officer and the Municipal Accountant coordinate to update reporting and accounting guidelines for the local road network.

We recommended that the Management prepare and updated physical inventory report of all its properties. We also recommended that the General Services Officer/Property Custodian maintain property records.

We recommended that Management prioritize the  immediate operation of said X-ray machine. The Management should properly plan its undertakings and consider all necessary measures before the implementation/procurement thereof to ensure efficient and effective delivery of services to its constituents.

We recommended that the Petty Cash Fund Custodian return immediately to the Collecting Officer of the LGU her unutilized petty cash fund amounting P23,697.91 to fully liquidate her cash advance that has been idle already for more than 8 months. We also recommended that Management comply with the proper use of Petty Cash Fund as provided under COA Circular 97-002.

We recommended that Management:
Require the contractor to immediately construct the concrete panel box, otherwise, refund the payment made to him corresponding to the cost of uninstalled panel box.
The uninstalled 25 KVA transformer will be used in the Community Hospital to support the newly purchased X-ray machine which were not put in operation due to lack of transformer.

We recommended that Management comply strictly with COA Circular No. 2009-001 and COA Circular No. 94-006 such as:
1.  Furnish the Auditor copy of contract (including purchase orders and memorandum of agreement) and each of all the documents forming part thereof by reference of incorporation within five (5) working days from the execution of contract; and
2. Notify the Auditor within 24 hours from accepting deliveries of procured items.

We recommended the following:
Management strictly enforce the provisions of Section 33 of RA 7160 for the execution of a Memorandum of Agreement which includes among others, project name, intended beneficiaries, amount of fund based on the cost estimates, brief description of the project, location, and systems and procedures to implement the project.
Record the transfer of funds under the inter-agency receivables account Due from LGUs in order to establish accountability and responsibility and to ensure that the transfer is properly taken up in the books of both agencies.
Management establish specific guidelines and policies in the grant of all kinds of financial assistance.

We recommend that the management should strictly observe the General Provision of General Appropriations Act for FY 2018 which provides a limitation on the net take home pay of officials and employees of the government in the amount not below P5,000.00.

We recommended that the Management ensure that the 20% DF of the LGU be allotted and used for priority development programs, projects and activities which shall contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic, development and environmental management as prescribe under DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017.

We recommended that the Management evaluate the status of the project implementation and identify the reason for the delay in the completion of the projects funded by the NGAs and to fast track the implementation of the programs/projects for the benefit of the constituents.  We also recommended that the financial assistance from the Province of Isabela be reclassified to its proper account Due to LGUs.

1.3	We recommended that Management undertake (1) a complete periodic physical inventory of municipal properties, (2) submit the same to the Auditor and (3) immediately reconcile the balances with accounting records to permit fair presentation of PPE in the financial statements.
2.6	We recommend that management comply with the provisions of RA No. 9184 prohibiting splitting of contracts.

3.4	We recommended that management adhere to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2007-001 and make representations to the NGOs/POs for the immediate liquidation of the Due from Non-Government Organizations amounting to P78,500.00.
4.3	We recommended management to monitor the progress of the implementation of the remaining project still in progress and ensure its earliest possible accomplishment.
5.4	We recommended management to monitor the progress of the implementation of the projects and ensure its earliest possible accomplishment.
6.4	We recommended for management to revisit the program and explore the possibility of its full implementation by coordination with the source agency.

7.4	We recommended for management to:
1.	Revert the unexpended balance of the BuB to the source agency; or
2.	Explore the legal possibility for the full implementation of the project by making adequate representation with the source agency.

8.5	We recommended that management (1) revisit and continue implementing the measures contained in the Municipality’s 10-Year SWM Plan, (2) fast tract the implementation of SLF and (3) continue adopting such measures to comply with the mandate of the law.

9.4	We recommended for management through the property disposal committee to (1) conduct inventory and appraise the value of the unserviceable properties; and (2) immediately dispose the same in accordance with existing regulation.

10.6	We recommended that management (1) to adhere to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-003 and be cautious in spending government funds and (2) refund the excess amount paid for the conduct of training.
11.6	We recommended that disbursements under the 20% Development Fund be consistent with DBM and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 17-1.

12.3	We recommended for management to fast tract the implementation of all on – going and not yet started programmed and funded projects under the 20% Development Fund to optimize utilization thereof and timely brings the desired benefits to the intended beneficiaries.

13.4	We recommended for management to fast tract the implementation of all on – going and not yet started programmed and funded projects under the LDRRM Fund to optimize utilization thereof and strengthen its disaster risk reduction and management system.

14.4	We recommended that Management continue to reinforce efforts on the collection of audit disallowances as required under Sections 5.4 and 7.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-006 and necessary documentations on the actions of the Municipality must be complied, copy furnish the Office of the Auditor.
15.5	We recommended for management to continuously comply with all regulations pertaining to GAD advocacies.

16.5	We recommended that management continue the practice of depositing collections regularly to comply with the rules and regulations as mandated by Section 69 of P.D. No. 1445 and Section 32 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I on collections and deposits.

17.4	We recommended that the Accountable Officer continue the proper and regular monitoring of the accountable forms so that information as to the receipts, issuances and balances in her custody can be readily determined through preparation of the Monthly Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms (RAAF).

18.3	We recommended that the Accountable Officer monitor the proper use of the petty cash so that such fund will not be abused. Also, to avoid use of personal money, the AO may request replenishment of the cash advance when its disbursements reach at least 75% or as the need requires.

19.6	We recommended that the Accountable Officer:
a.	Restitute the cash shortage amounting to P499.45;
b.	Stop the unsound practices of handling cash; and
c.	Comply with the aforementioned provisions of P.D. No. 1445.
20.4	We recommended for management to submit to DOH the liquidation papers pertaining to the amount of P742,000.31 already adjusted in the books of the municipality.

21.4	We recommended that management prepare and submit its CY 2017 Agency Action Plan and Status of Implementation (AAPSI) within the period prescribed by the law using the prescribed form under COA Memorandum No. 2014 – 002 dated March 18, 2014. It is further recommended that a focal person be designated by the Local Chief Executive who will consolidate the report before submission to the audit team.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the (a) Municipal Treasurer to update the RPTAR and submit a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year to the Accounting Unit to be used as basis in setting up of RPT/SET receivable as required by Section 20 of Manual on NGAS for LGUs; and (b) Municipal Accountant to observe the aforementioned provisions as the basis in recording the RPT/SET receivables at the beginning of the year.

We recommended and the Management agreed to establish effective measures to improve cash position in order to transfer unexpended balances of Local Disaster Risk Reduction Fund to a special trust fund to ensure its availability during times of disaster/calamity.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to (a) investigate the occurrence of negative/debit balances in the liability accounts and make the necessary adjustments in the books; and (b) refund to source agency or remit to Bureau of Treasury the unexpended balances of transferred funds/completed projects as prescribed by COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to (a) instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to (i) reconcile the discrepancy between the General and Subsidiary Ledger balances of PPE accounts; and (ii) adjust the overstated depreciation of Other PPE account; (b) create an Inventory Committee who will conduct physical count of properties of the Municipality and will prepare the Report on Physical Count of Plant, Property and Equipment (RCPPE); and (c) instruct Municipal Treasurer to maintain property cards and other records such as Property Acknowledgement Receipts and inventory tags.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to make a detailed review of the two reciprocal accounts and determine the cause of the unreconciled amounts.  Thereafter, make the necessary adjustments/corrections in the books of accounts to present the correct balances of the affected accounts.

We recommended and the municipal officials agreed to continue to administer the funds properly and to continue to limit expenditures to the essential operating needs of the municipality in order to further reduce and eventually eliminate cash deficit.

We reiterated our prior years’ recommendations and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the OIC-Municipal Treasurer and OIC-Municipal Accountant to (a) make concerted effort to determine the causes of delay in the submission of accounts and plan and establish an effective measure on how to resolve it; and (b) expedite the submission of still unsubmitted accounts such as the
official receipts, disbursement vouchers, payrolls, trial balance and supporting schedules to the Office of the Auditor.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to provide justification on the delayed submission of SAAOB covering the period CY 2018, henceforth, ensure that the said report are submitted within 60 days after the 31st of December of each year as required by COA Accounting Circular Letter No. 2007-002.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive and the MPDC agreed to (a) refrain from including expenditure items that are not allowed to be charged against the Development Fund (DF); and (b) optimally utilize budget for Development Fund.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive and Local School Board (LSB) agreed to (a) judiciously prepare SEF budget according to the needs of the stakeholders and consider the reprogramming/realignment of unutilized funds so that more projects could be implemented by the Municipality for the benefits of the intended users; and (b) comply with the reportorial requirements of DepEd, DBM and DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017, dated January 19, 2017.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive and the LDRRMO agreed to (a) strictly monitor the implementation of all program/projects/activities in the LDRRMP in order to strengthen the disaster and mitigation measures of the Municipality which will be beneficial in times of calamities; and (b) comply with the reportorial requirements of JMC No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013.

We recommended and the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board (MSWMB) agreed to consider prioritizing the establishment of Municipal MRF and purchase of recycling and composting equipment as stated in the Ten-Year Solid Waste Management of the Municipality in order to further contribute in the total protection of the environment and general welfare of the constituents.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to (a) instruct the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to regularly conduct examination on the books of accounts of business establishments to determine the truthfulness of the declared gross sales/receipts used in the computation of business taxes paid and to collect the correct taxes as provided under Section 171 of R.A. 7160;  (b) assign personnel at the MTO who are equip with the necessary knowledge and training in the examination of books of accounts to enable said Office in carrying on with their mandate efficiently; and (c) instruct the BPLO to require taxpayers to submit Sworn Statement of Gross Receipts or Sales and Certified Copy of Income Tax Return to verify or assess the correct tax base to be used as provided by Section 2C.04 (d) and (e) of the Revised Revenue Code of the Municipality of Quezon, Nueva Ecija.

We recommended that Local Chief Executive direct the Executive Committee of the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) to (a) ensure the implementation of GAD programs and activities in accordance with approved GAD plans and budget and (b) refrain from including programs which cannot be identified with GAD.

We recommended that the management continue complying with the BIR rules and regulations on the withholding and remittance of withholding taxes on compensation and value added taxes, percentage taxes and expanded taxes from government money payments to assure that the government was not deprived of taxes that can be used in the implementation of various programs and projects.

We recommended that the management continue complying with the GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG regulations on the withholding and remittance of employees’ and employer contributions and loan amortizations of employees to the respective agencies and remit fully the remaining balance of due to GSIS to assure all its members of the social security benefits due them.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the Inventory
Committee to submit the duly accomplished RPCPPE to the Audit Team, and the
GSO-Designate to prepare and maintain the property cards and reconcile the same
with the PPELCs of the Accounting Office. Furthermore, observe the provisions of
Section 124 of the MNGAS, Volume I on the prescribed timeline in the submission
of RPCPPE to allow the Audit Team the necessary verification and reconciliation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Assessor to fast-
track the on-going general revision of real properties in order to provide the MT the
complete and updated Assessment Roll and the MT to submit to the Municipal
Accountant the certified list of taxpayers showing their names and amounts due
and collectible for the year so that the RPT/SET Receivables can be adjusted to
reflect the correct balances as of December 31, 2018.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant to confirm from the account owners/creditors and to request them to
submit documents pertaining to their claims to the Municipality. In the event that
they fail to support their claims, revert the account to the Unappropriated Surplus
of the General Fund, subject to an authority from the SB through a Resolution with
a condition that appropriation shall be made in the event that claim arises in the
future pursuant to the provisions of Section 98 of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to
continue her efforts in providing the details/breakdown of the said accounts and
remit the same to the aforementioned Agencies so that member beneficiaries may
avail of the privileges and benefits due them.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to
settle immediately her outstanding cash advance. Otherwise, effect the withholding
of her salary in accordance with COA Circular No. 97-002. Henceforth, see to it that
all cash advances are liquidated at the end of each year.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the General Services Officer-
Designate to expedite the compliance with the recommendation of the GSIS
pertaining to the assessment of the insurable properties and the provision of its
insurance.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Assessor to:
a. Fast-track the titling of the three lots whose titling process were already
commenced in CY 2018, and
b. Make all the necessary actions/representations with the DENR and RD in the
titling of the other 11 lots.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to require the negotiating committee to
expedite the negotiation with the lot owners to resolve the right-of-way issue so that
the said project will be implemented for the benefit of the constituents.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Treasurer to strictly adhere with the regulations and deposit all municipal
collections promptly and intact in order to protect municipal funds against risk of
loss and possible misuse.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Treasurer exhaust
all possible means to locate and secure the documents to support the unrecorded
disbursements and immediately transmit the documents to the Municipal
Accountant to facilitate recording thereof in the book of accounts and subsequent
submission to the Audit Team.

We also reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant
give the details of the said disbursement as reflected in the Bank Reconciliation
Statements and forward a certified copy of the cleared checks to the Audit Team
for our reference in audit.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Treasurer send
written notice to the concerned payees at least one month before a check becomes
stale. We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer verify in the Check
Registers the recipients/signatory of those checks when released by their Office,
otherwise, make the necessary adjustments if the same were unreleased checks.

Moreover, we advised that the Municipal Accountant make the necessary
adjustments in the books for the long outstanding/stale checks in accordance with
Section 59 of the NGAS Manual, Volume I.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer obtain the necessary
details of the transactions like Memorandum of Agreement, bank credit
memoranda, and related communications from the source of the fund and
forward the same to the Municipal Accountant to facilitate recording in the books.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer maintain the prescribed
cashbook, keep it updated and reconcile her cashbook against the Subsidiary
Ledgers of the Municipal Accountant for both the Cash in Local Treasury and
Cash in Bank accounts every end of the month.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant furnish the
Municipal Treasurer with the copies of Subsidiary Ledgers, Bank Statements and
Bank Reconciliation Statements for her reference in reconciling her cashbooks
with accounting records, and issue a certification on the balances of all
Accountable Officers who maintain a cashbook for their reference in its
reconciliation against the SLs.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor thru the Municipal
Accountant send demand letter to concerned Accountable Officers, who are still
connected with the Municipality, to liquidate their cash advances and henceforth
strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor appoint another DO to
unload and help the current DO.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the previous DO to
reconcile her accountabilities against the records of the Municipal Accountant
and settle any remaining balances, otherwise file a case against the DO, if
warranted. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant send demand letter
to the former DO.

We also recommended that the Administrative Officer II of the accounting
office submit the reconciliation report and the DO transmit a certified copy of her
cashbook to the Audit Team for reference in audit.

We reiterated our recommendation that the:
a. GSO and the Municipal Engineer complete the conduct of physical inventory
count of the remaining PPE of the Municipality and submit the Report on
Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment in the prescribed form with
acquisition costs and total;
b. GSO and the Municipal Accountant reconcile the Report on the Physical
Count of PPE, Property Cards, Property Ledger Cards, and accounting
records, taking into consideration the noted deficiencies in audit, and furnish
the Audit Team with the said report; and
c. GSO submit an Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property
(IIRUP) with transportation equipment’s checklist, pictures and in-house
appraisal values.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Management facilitate the
transfer of title of the real properties under the name of the Municipal
Government of Quezon to prevent future dispute of ownership.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant maintain the Real
Property Ledger Cards for all the real properties of the Municipality with
complete details to determine any difference between the records of the Municipal
Assessor and financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant recognize in the books
the Local Roads inventoried by the Municipal Engineer and adequately disclose
the Road Network System in the Notes to Financial Statements.

We recommended that the:
a. Sangguniang Bayan of Quezon amend the Revised Revenue Code to conform
with the Tourism Code with regards to the utilization of eco-
tourism/environmental fee;
b. Municipal Budget Officer remove in the Annual Budget of the Municipality the
projected receipts of the eco-tourism/ environmental fee;
c. Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant account for this fund
under the Trust Fund and transfer fees collected in CY 2018 to the Trust Fund
of the Municipality; and
d. Tourism Officer (i) submit a receipt and utilization report to the Audit Team
for monitoring purposes; and (ii) properly coordinate with the concerned
Barangays to mitigate the carbon footprints of tourists/visitors by undertaking
projects and programs that will address these issues.

We recommended that the HRMO:
a. Maintain a hand/written copy of PICLA for each permanent/regular
employee and update/record leave credits and its availment on a monthly
basis;
b. Exercise prudence in the posting and computation of entries in the PICLA; and
c. Prepare summary of leave credit balances of all employees at the end of the
year duly attested by each employee and post it in bulletin boards for their
reference.

We recommended to require the Local Accountant to adjust immediately the reconciling items found in the Monthly Bank Reconciliation Statement such as bank interest and tax, unrecorded disbursement and deposits for this ensures prompt detection of discrepancies, the parties at fault, information of their existence and timely identification and application of proper action/remedy to correct them. Also, confirm to the Local Treasurer the status of the checks if still outstanding over six months and prepare appropriate adjusting entries to correct the same. In addition, record deposits only when actually deposited and not as when collected to accurately account the actual cash in bank and on hand. Further, prepare adjusting entry to reclassify the Cash in Bank- Local Currency Time Deposit account to Cash in Bank- Local Currency Savings account.
Require the Local Treasurer to prepare checks only when funds are available so as not to incur bank overdraft and possible bank penalties for bouncing checks.

We recommended to require the Municipal Treasurer and Liquidating Officer to diligently monitor the remittance of cash collections received by the collecting officers. Collections should be cut-off at the end of the month to report collections made for the period. Also, require the accountable officers to submit monthly individual RAAFs as basis in the preparation of monthly Consolidated RAAF. Review the RAAF and CRAAF if the data reflected are correct and without errors before submission to COA. Moreover, the Municipal Treasurer should verify and sign the Report of Collections and Deposits prepared by the Liquidating Officer to establish a good internal control for cash management.

We recommended the Management to direct the Inventory Committee and the concerned offices to exert extra efforts in reconciling the reports per physical inventory, per GSO and per accounting records.
Expedite the disposition of all unserviceable and obsolete properties for the municipality to avoid further deterioration and to generate additional income from their disposal. Specifically, the Management should create Appraisal and Disposal Committees to undertake the appraisal and disposition of these properties in accordance to COA Circular No. 89-296 dated January 27, 1989 and PD 1445. Moreover, a request for audit action should be submitted to COA audit team before the conduct of proper disposal activity. The reports prepared should be furnished to the Municipal Accountant who shall prepare journal vouchers to drop the properties from the books of account.
The General Services Office to continue establishing good practices on supply and property management by extending more efforts to locate missing properties, by keeping updated records for property accountabilities and by demanding turn-over of either unserviceable properties from present accountable officers (AOs) or unused properties from resigned/retired/separated accountable officers.
Further, direct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the “unidentified beginning balances” subsidiary ledgers of PPE accounts to establish reliability and accuracy of accounts reflected in financial statements.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the Municipal Accountant to create detailed subsidiary ledger to support payable accounts to comply with procedural guidelines under Section 114 of PD 1445. Also, prepare aging schedule on a periodic basis to facilitate monitoring and evaluation of the accounts, indicating the status thereof.
Likewise, the Municipal Accountant is further advised to exercise caution in certifying existence of obligation and make periodic review of the outstanding payables.

We recommended to direct the Municipal Accountant to reissue demand letters and exhaust all possible remedies to collect the receivables and to demand liquidation of cash advances and fund transfers. Conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of these accounts. Comply with all the conditions and requirements of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 for the possible request for the write off of the specified dormant receivables and cash advances accounts to the Commission on Audit. Provide allowance for impairment thereafter and reconcile the accounts with unidentified/ unreconciled subsidiary ledgers from prior years in order to have a fair presentation of affected accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended management that financial assistance be properly handled by the Municipal Social Welfare Office for proper assessment of each case and the appropriateness of the documents presented vis-à-vis the requirements. A case study shall then be prepared as basis for payment.

We recommended to require the Bids and Awards Committee to include in its schedule of activities the procurement of fuel, oil and lubricants to be procured through public bidding. Publish the bid in the Phil-GEPS website to promote transparency and competitiveness to notify possible interested bidders who can afford to provide the most advantageous contract price, discounts and additional service offers.
The Municipal Accountant should also assure that disbursement vouchers are supported with adequate documents to avoid disallowance or suspension of transactions.
The Municipal Mayor and the Municipal Accountant should see to it that authorized passengers are indicated in the vehicle trip tickets, including the purpose of travel, to establish validity of request for gasoline and to make accountable those personnel who claims fare and gasoline at the same time, thus to avoid possible abuse on these transactions.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer to be more prudent in the grant of honoraria taking into consideration the limitations provided under Budget Circular 2007-1 and 2007-2.
Assign an individual to review activity design for training and determine what is most advantageous to the municipality.
Direct all persons liable to cause the refund of the honoraria/incentive in the total amount of P6,922,617.01 received by the municipal officials and employees of the municipality and other designated members of various committees.

We recommended management to require the submission of the written conformity and acquiescence of the Solicitor General or the Government Corporate Counsel, as the case maybe, and the written concurrence of the Commission on Audit shall first be secured before the hiring or employment of a private lawyer or law firm.

We recommend that the municipal accountant and municipal budget officer to be more discreet in the grant of BAC incentives/honorarium in accordance with the guidelines set by the DBM. Stop the practice of granting BAC incentives from the general fund which is bereft of legal basis.

The ITDO has been advised to present in the Computerized Official Receipt, the gross amount of Basic Tax and Special Education Tax and their corresponding discounts/penalties. Likewise, the ITDO has been advised to enhance the RPT System modules to generate reports indicating all significant information relevant to collection of Basic Tax and Special Education Tax for ready reference of the end-users.
observations.managementAction: OR submitted to COA

observations.recommendation:
The City Accountant has been advised to effect adjusting entries to correct the receivable account, Due from NGAs (Code 136) for unrealizable receivables from the MMDA due to the erroneous entries made in recording the City’s contributions to the said agency. In such manner, the balances of the accounts, Due from NGAs (Code 136) and Government Equity (Code 501) will be presented fairly in the financial statements.

However, in case an over-remittance to MMDA is established, the amount should be retained under receivable account and management is advised to make representation with the said agency for reconciliation of records to possibly recover the over-remittance made.   

Considering that the five percent MMDA share is a statutory obligation in equal monthly contribution, the City Budget Officer was advised to issue the Advice of Allotment on quarterly basis so that the City Accountant can record the contribution to expense account upon receipt of the Notice of Funding Check Issued without undue delay. Moreover, the City Budget Officer was advised to make timely proposal for realignment/augmentation of appropriations to cover any deficiency in the appropriation for all statutory obligations.

observations.recommendation: New Revenue Code CY 2018 to be released in 2019

The DBO trace and verify the non-issuance of Occupancy Permits to owners/engineers of buildings/structures with Building Permits issued in 2012 to 2014; and

The DBO strictly and regularly monitor the completed buildings/structures with Building Permits; and require the authorized engineers of the said constructions to submit the Certificate of Completion thereon for the issuance of Occupancy Permits and the payments of the required fees and penalties (if any) therefrom, pursuant to the  provisions of the Revenue Code and the National Building Code.

Only a small number of the completed buildings/structures with issued Occupancy Permits were included in the Assessment Roll of the City Assessor’s Office (CAO) as of October 24, 2015 for the collection of real property tax. However, the CAO had reported several assessments on properties without Building Permits which also deprived the City of income for the non-collection of the required fees and taxes thereon. 

We have recommended that the Building Official see to it that the requirement of the Executive Order is strictly complied with on the presentation of the Tax Declaration by the applicant prior to the release of the Occupancy Permit.

The City Assessor review the schedule submitted by the DBO on the buildings/structures with Occupancy Permits and make sure that all those constructions supposed to have been completed by this time are already assessed for taxation purposes.

Henceforth, the City Assessor ensure that all properties with issued Tax Declarations and Occupancy Permits are incorporated in the Assessment Roll to be submitted to the City Treasurer’s Office for the collection of the required real property taxes thereon; and

The DBO and City Assessor’s Office coordinate closely to assure the strict implementation of the rules and regulations on Occupancy Permit and assessment of real property tax.

Further, we have recommended that the Management: 

Ascertain the reasons why there were structures constructed without the application/payment of the required Building Permit;

Identify the personnel assigned in the respective areas where assessments were made to structures without Building Permits and hold them responsible for such deficiencies, if warranted; 

Review the present systems and existing rules and regulations of the City in the issuance of Building Permit and Occupancy Permit; and assessment of real property tax; and

See to it that the DBO and CAO coordinate closely to assure the strict implementation of the rules and regulations on Building Permit, Occupancy Permit in accordance with the National Building Code; and assessment of real property tax.

Issuance of Occupancy Permits to structures which are not in accordance with the provisions of the National Building Code

We have recommended that the Building Official institute control procedures to make sure that all issuance of Building Permits and Occupancy Permits are strictly in accordance with the provisions of the National Building Code. There should be proper monitoring of the actions of the DBO in the implementation thereof. Violations noted should be properly rectified or penalized as prescribed in the Code.

Fact is when the City government assumed the operational and management aspect of the QMC in 2008, the City has preserved the Quezon Memorial Shrine's sanctity. We resisted vertical constructions near the museum's surrounding areas. To complement the ambiance at the said museum, various gardens surrounding the NHI property were put up by the City such as Flower Garden, Fern Garden, Rock Garden and Tropical Garden. The dynamic lighting at the museum's pylon was revived - its cost of repair wqas funded by the City government. observations.recommendation:
The Local Finance Committee and the Budget Department, as the lead Office:

•    Conduct a systematic and realistic forecasting of revenue taking into account prior years’ experience and statistics as aids in setting targets;

•    Review the present budgeting to set a clearer guide for the concerned revenue generating Departments towards attaining the targets set; and

•    Provide for the targets on accounts that did not have projections which should be based on statistics, prior year’s experience and future expectations.

The City Treasury and other Departments involved in generating revenues endeavor to meet its targets.  Periodic review should be made by the concerned officials in the City to ascertain whether collection targets are attained.  The reasons for the shortfall in collections should be analyzed and alternative actions should be looked into to avoid incurrence of misses in projections in the future;

The Local Finance Committee complement the effort of the City Treasury in monitoring revenue targets. Periodic review should be conducted to check the performance level and also to ascertain remedial measures necessary for revenue accounts that missed the projections and procedures that will improve more the collections of taxes that posts good results; and

The Budget Department correct the coding of projected revenues to align and conform with the Standard Chart of Accounts promulgated by the Commission on Audit pursuant to COA Circular No. COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated 01 December 2015.
